questions.question: Someone says "How are you?" — what is the most common reply?
questions.explanation: "I am fine, thank you" (or "I'm fine, thanks") is the most common reply to "How are you?"

questions.question: You meet someone for the first time. What do you say?
questions.explanation: "Nice to meet you" is the standard phrase when meeting someone for the first time.

questions.question: Complete: "Good ___, how are you today?" (morning / night / bye)
questions.explanation: "Good morning" is used when greeting someone in the morning.

questions.question: It is 8pm. Which greeting is correct?
questions.explanation: "Good evening" is used from about 6pm onwards as a greeting.

questions.question: What does "Good night" mean?
options: Something you say when leaving or going to sleep
A way to say thank you
questions.explanation: "Good night" is said when leaving at night or when going to bed — not a greeting.

questions.question: "What is your ___?" means someone wants to know your name. (job / name / age)
questions.explanation: "What is your name?" is how you ask someone's name.

questions.question: Someone says "Nice to meet you." What do you reply?
questions.explanation: Reply to "Nice to meet you" with "Nice to meet you too."

questions.question: Which is an informal way to say hello?
questions.explanation: "Hi" is informal and casual. "Good morning/evening" and "How do you do" are more formal.

questions.question: "___ do you do?" is a very formal British greeting. (How / What / Where)
questions.explanation: "How do you do?" is a formal British greeting, usually replied to with "How do you do?"

questions.question: You are leaving a friend. What do you say?
questions.explanation: "See you later!" is a common informal goodbye.

questions.question: "I am from Myanmar." — What question was asked?
questions.explanation: "Where are you from?" asks about someone's country or city of origin.

questions.question: "___ to meet you" — complete the greeting. (Good / Nice / Hello)
questions.explanation: "Nice to meet you" is the standard first-meeting phrase.

questions.question: Which phrase means "goodbye" in a formal situation?
questions.explanation: "Farewell" is a formal and somewhat literary way to say goodbye.

questions.question: Someone asks "How are you doing?" — which is NOT a good reply?
questions.explanation: "Goodbye" is not a reply to "How are you doing?" — it means you are leaving.

questions.question: "What do you ___?" asks about someone's job. (do / want / like)
questions.explanation: "What do you do?" is the common way to ask about someone's job or occupation.

questions.question: Which greeting is used between 12pm and 6pm?
questions.explanation: "Good afternoon" is used from noon (12pm) until early evening (around 6pm).

questions.question: "Long time no see!" means:
options: I have not seen you in a long time.
I am happy to meet you.
questions.explanation: "Long time no see!" is an informal expression meaning you haven't seen that person for a long time.

questions.question: "___ are you?" is the most common greeting question. (How / What / Where)
questions.explanation: "How are you?" is the most common everyday greeting question.

questions.question: Someone sneezes. What do you say?
questions.explanation: "Bless you!" is what English speakers say when someone sneezes.

questions.question: "I'm doing well, thank you." — What question was asked?
questions.explanation: "I'm doing well" is a reply to "How are you?"

questions.question: You want someone to repeat what they said. What do you say?
questions.explanation: "Could you repeat that, please?" is the polite way to ask someone to say something again.

questions.question: "Excuse ___" is used to politely get someone's attention. (me / you / him)
questions.explanation: "Excuse me" is used to politely get attention or to apologize for a small interruption.

questions.question: You want to pass someone in a crowded place. What do you say?
options: Excuse me, could I get past?
questions.explanation: "Excuse me, could I get past?" is the polite way to ask someone to move.

questions.question: "Could you speak more slowly, please?" — Why would you say this?
options: The person is speaking too quietly.
The person is speaking too fast.
The person is speaking a different language.
questions.explanation: You ask someone to speak more slowly when they are talking too fast for you to understand.

questions.question: "___ you mind opening the window?" is a very polite request. (Would / Will / Can)
questions.explanation: "Would you mind...?" is a very polite way to make a request.

questions.question: Someone holds the door for you. What do you say?
questions.explanation: "Thank you!" is the correct response when someone does something kind for you.

questions.question: Someone says "Thank you." What do you reply?
questions.explanation: "You're welcome" is the standard reply to "Thank you."

questions.question: "I'm ___" is what you say when you make a mistake. (sorry / please / fine)
questions.explanation: "I'm sorry" is the standard apology in English.

questions.question: You accidentally bump into someone. What do you say?
questions.explanation: "I'm sorry!" is what you say when you accidentally do something wrong.

questions.question: Someone says "I'm sorry." What is a kind reply?
questions.explanation: "That's okay, don't worry" is a kind and forgiving reply to an apology.

questions.question: "Can you ___ me?" means you need help. (help / see / find)
questions.explanation: "Can you help me?" is a simple and direct request for assistance.

questions.question: Which is the most polite way to ask for something?
options: Could I have some water, please?
questions.explanation: "Could I have...please?" is the most polite form of requesting something.

questions.question: "No problem!" means:
options: It is okay, I am happy to help.
questions.explanation: "No problem!" means you are happy to help and it was not difficult or inconvenient.

questions.question: "___ you tell me where the bathroom is?" (Can / Am / Do)
questions.explanation: "Can you tell me...?" is a polite way to ask for directions or information.

questions.question: You do not understand something. What do you say?
questions.explanation: "I'm sorry, I don't understand" is the polite way to say you did not understand.

questions.question: "Of course!" means:
questions.explanation: "Of course!" means yes, definitely — used to agree or confirm enthusiastically.

questions.question: "___ I sit here?" asks for permission. (Can / Do / Am)
questions.explanation: "Can I sit here?" or "May I sit here?" asks politely for permission.

questions.question: Someone asks "Do you need help?" but you are fine. What do you say?
questions.explanation: "No thank you, I'm okay" politely declines help when you don't need it.

questions.question: "Just a moment, please." means:
questions.explanation: "Just a moment" means please wait a very short time.

questions.question: "___ is very kind of you." means thank you in a formal way. (That / It / This)
questions.explanation: "That is very kind of you" is a formal and warm way to express thanks.

questions.question: You enter a shop. The assistant says "Can I help you?" What do you say?
options: No, I am just looking, thank you.
questions.explanation: "I'm just looking, thank you" is a polite way to say you don't need help yet.

questions.question: "How ___ is this?" asks about the price. (much / many / long)
questions.explanation: "How much is this?" asks for the price of something.

questions.question: You want to pay. What do you say?
options: I would like to pay, please.
Do you have this in blue?
questions.explanation: "I would like to pay, please" is the polite way to ask for the bill or to pay.

questions.question: "Do you have this in a different size?" — Where would you say this?
questions.explanation: You ask about different sizes in a clothes shop when trying on clothing.

questions.question: "I'd like to ___ this, please." means you want to buy it. (have / buy / take)
questions.explanation: "I'd like to take this" or "I'll take this" is a common way to say you want to buy something.

questions.question: The cashier says "That's $10.50." What do you do?
options: Give the correct amount or a card.
questions.explanation: When told the price, you pay the amount in cash or by card.

questions.question: "Can I get a receipt, please?" — What is a receipt?
options: A paper showing what you bought and paid
questions.explanation: A receipt is a paper or digital record of what you bought and how much you paid.

questions.question: "Do you ___ credit cards?" asks about payment methods. (accept / have / want)
questions.explanation: "Do you accept credit cards?" asks if you can pay by card.

questions.question: "This is too expensive. Do you have anything cheaper?" — What are you doing?
options: Looking for a lower price option
questions.explanation: You are asking for a cheaper alternative — a common shopping phrase.

questions.question: You want to return something you bought. What do you say?
options: I would like a refund, please.
questions.explanation: "I would like a refund" is what you say when returning a product and wanting your money back.

questions.question: "Where is the ___ room?" asks where to try on clothes. (fitting / waiting / rest)
questions.explanation: A fitting room (or changing room) is where you try on clothes in a shop.

questions.question: "It's on sale" means:
questions.explanation: "On sale" means the product is being sold at a reduced/discounted price.

questions.question: You need medicine. Where do you go?
questions.explanation: A pharmacy (or chemist in British English) is where you buy medicine.

questions.question: "I have a ___." tells a doctor what is wrong with you. (problem / headache / question)
questions.explanation: Common phrase at a doctor: "I have a headache / stomachache / fever."

questions.question: "Table for two, please." — Where do you say this?
questions.explanation: "Table for two" is said at a restaurant when you want a table for 2 people.

questions.question: "Can I see the menu, please?" — What is a menu?
options: A list of food and drinks available
questions.explanation: A menu is a list of all the food and drinks available at a restaurant.

questions.question: "I'll have the ___." is how you order food at a restaurant. (same / bill / chicken)
questions.explanation: "I'll have the chicken / pasta / soup" is the standard way to order food.

questions.question: "The bill, please." — When do you say this at a restaurant?
options: When you want to pay and leave
questions.explanation: "The bill, please" is said when you want to pay and leave the restaurant.

questions.question: "Is ___ included?" asks if you need to pay extra for service. (service / tax / tip)
questions.explanation: "Is service included?" asks whether the tip/service charge is already in the bill.

questions.question: "Sold out" means:
questions.explanation: "Sold out" means all the stock has been sold and there is nothing left.

questions.question: "Excuse me, how do I get to the train station?" — What are you asking?
options: The price of a train ticket
The way to the train station
The time of the next train
questions.explanation: "How do I get to...?" asks for directions to a place.

questions.question: "Turn ___ at the traffic lights." (left / up / over)
questions.explanation: "Turn left" or "Turn right" are basic direction instructions.

questions.question: "Go straight ahead." means:
questions.explanation: "Go straight ahead" means keep walking or driving without turning.

questions.question: "It's on your left." means the place is:
questions.explanation: "On your left" means the place is on the left side as you face forward.

questions.question: "It's ___ the corner." means just after you turn. (around / under / inside)
questions.explanation: "Around the corner" means very close, just after you turn the corner.

questions.question: "How far is it?" asks about:
questions.explanation: "How far is it?" asks about the distance to a place.

questions.question: "It's about a 10-minute walk." means:
options: You need to take a bus.
questions.explanation: "A 10-minute walk" means you can walk there in about 10 minutes.

questions.question: "Take the ___ left." means the second road on the left. (second / next / first)
questions.explanation: "Take the second left" means pass the first road and turn at the second one.

questions.question: "I'm lost." means:
options: I cannot find where I am.
questions.explanation: "I'm lost" means you don't know where you are or how to get to your destination.

questions.question: "It's next to the post office." means:
questions.explanation: "Next to" means beside, very close and adjacent to something.

questions.question: "___ this road until you see the park." (Follow / Take / Go)
questions.explanation: "Follow this road" means stay on the same road without turning.

questions.question: "You can't miss it!" means:
options: It is very easy to find.
questions.explanation: "You can't miss it!" means it is very obvious and easy to find.

questions.question: Someone says "It's opposite the bank." Where is the place?
questions.explanation: "Opposite" means directly across from something, facing it.

questions.question: "___ at the end of the road." (Turn / Stop / It's)
questions.explanation: "It's at the end of the road" tells you the location is at the very end.

questions.question: "Is it within walking distance?" means:
options: Can I walk there (is it close enough)?
questions.explanation: "Within walking distance" means close enough to walk to without transport.

questions.question: "Take the number 5 bus." — What is this?
options: An instruction to take a specific bus
questions.explanation: "Take the number 5 bus" means get on the bus with the number 5 route.

questions.question: "___ right at the roundabout." (Turn / Go / Take)
questions.explanation: "Turn right at the roundabout" is a common direction instruction.

questions.question: "The nearest underground station is Oxford Street." — What is "underground"?
questions.explanation: In British English, "underground" (or "the Tube") is the subway/metro train system.

questions.question: "It's a 5-minute drive." means:
questions.explanation: "A 5-minute drive" means the journey by car takes about 5 minutes.

questions.question: "Go ___ the bridge and it's on your right." (over / under / around)
questions.explanation: "Go over the bridge" means cross the bridge to the other side.

questions.question: Your mother's mother is your:
questions.explanation: Your mother's mother (or father's mother) is your grandmother.

questions.question: Your father's brother is your:
questions.explanation: Your father's or mother's brother is your uncle.

questions.question: Your brother's son is your ___. (nephew / cousin / son)
questions.explanation: Your brother's or sister's son is your nephew.

questions.question: Your uncle's children are your:
questions.explanation: Your aunt's or uncle's children are your cousins.

questions.question: "Sibling" means:
questions.explanation: "Sibling" is the word for a brother or sister.

questions.question: Your sister's daughter is your ___. (niece / cousin / daughter)
questions.explanation: Your brother's or sister's daughter is your niece.

questions.question: Your husband or wife is your:
questions.explanation: Your husband or wife is called your spouse or partner.

questions.question: "In-laws" means:
questions.explanation: "In-laws" refers to your spouse's family — mother-in-law, father-in-law, etc.

questions.question: Your parents' parents are your ___. (grandparents / great-parents / ancestors)
questions.explanation: Your mother's and father's parents are your grandparents.

questions.question: "Only child" means:
options: A child with no brothers or sisters
questions.explanation: An "only child" is someone who has no brothers or sisters.

questions.question: Your father's new wife (not your mother) is your:
questions.explanation: Your father's wife who is not your biological mother is your stepmother.

questions.question: The oldest child in a family is the ___. (eldest / youngest / middle)
questions.explanation: The oldest child is called the eldest (or oldest) child.

questions.question: "Twins" means:
options: Two children born at different times
Two children born at the same time
questions.explanation: Twins are two children born from the same pregnancy at the same time.

questions.question: Your mother's sister is your:
questions.explanation: Your mother's or father's sister is your aunt.

questions.question: A person who has no parents is called an ___. (orphan / only child / elder)
questions.explanation: An orphan is a child whose parents have died.

questions.question: "Extended family" means:
options: Grandparents, aunts, uncles, cousins and more
questions.explanation: Extended family includes grandparents, aunts, uncles, cousins — beyond just parents and children.

questions.question: Your son's wife is your:
questions.explanation: Your son's wife is your daughter-in-law.

questions.question: Your children's children are your ___. (grandchildren / great-children / nephews)
questions.explanation: Your son's or daughter's children are your grandchildren.

questions.question: "Nuclear family" means:
options: Parents and their children living together
questions.explanation: A nuclear family is the basic unit: parents and their children.

questions.question: Your brother's wife is your:
questions.explanation: Your brother's wife is your sister-in-law.

questions.question: Which is a fruit?
questions.explanation: Mango is a fruit. Carrot, potato, and onion are vegetables.

questions.question: Which is a vegetable?
questions.explanation: Broccoli is a vegetable. Apple, banana, and strawberry are fruits.

questions.question: You drink this hot in the morning — it wakes you up. It is called ___. (coffee / juice / milk)
questions.explanation: Coffee is a popular hot drink that contains caffeine, which helps wake you up.

questions.question: "Dairy products" means food made from:
questions.explanation: Dairy products are made from milk — like cheese, butter, yogurt, and cream.

questions.question: Which is a grain?
questions.explanation: Rice is a grain. Grains include rice, wheat, oats, and corn.

questions.question: Bread is made from ___. (wheat / rice / corn)
questions.explanation: Most bread is made from wheat flour.

questions.question: "Seafood" includes:
questions.explanation: Seafood includes fish, shrimp, crab, squid, and other sea creatures.

questions.question: What does "boiled" mean?
questions.explanation: "Boiled" means cooked in boiling (very hot) water.

questions.question: Food that is not cooked is called ___. (raw / fresh / cold)
questions.explanation: "Raw" means not cooked — raw meat, raw vegetables.

questions.question: "Spicy" food is:
questions.explanation: "Spicy" means the food has a hot, sharp taste from chili or pepper.

questions.question: Which drink has no caffeine?
questions.explanation: Water has no caffeine. Coffee, black tea, and energy drinks all contain caffeine.

questions.question: A meal eaten in the middle of the day is called ___. (lunch / breakfast / dinner)
questions.explanation: Lunch is the meal eaten around midday (12pm).

questions.question: "Vegetarian" means someone who:
questions.explanation: A vegetarian does not eat meat (chicken, beef, pork, etc.) but may eat dairy and eggs.

questions.question: Which is a protein food?
questions.explanation: Eggs are high in protein. Other protein foods include meat, fish, beans, and tofu.

questions.question: Food that tastes like sugar is called ___. (sweet / sour / salty)
questions.explanation: "Sweet" describes food that tastes like sugar — cake, candy, fruit.

questions.question: "Grilled" means food cooked:
questions.explanation: "Grilled" means cooked directly over or under heat — like a barbecue or grill.

questions.question: Which is a breakfast food?
questions.explanation: Cereal with milk is a typical breakfast food in many countries.

questions.question: Lemon juice tastes ___. (sour / sweet / bitter)
questions.explanation: "Sour" describes a sharp, acidic taste — like lemon or vinegar.

questions.question: "Organic food" means:
questions.explanation: Organic food is grown naturally without synthetic pesticides or fertilizers.

questions.question: Coffee without sugar is called ___. (black / white / green)
questions.explanation: "Black coffee" is coffee with no milk or sugar added.

questions.question: How do you say 15 in words?
questions.explanation: 15 = fifteen. Note: not "fiveteen" — the correct spelling is fifteen.

questions.question: 20 in words is ___. (twenty / twety / two-ten)
questions.explanation: 20 = twenty. The -ty suffix is used for tens: twenty, thirty, forty, fifty...

questions.question: How do you say 1st?
questions.explanation: 1st = first. Ordinal numbers: 1st=first, 2nd=second, 3rd=third, 4th=fourth...

questions.question: "It's half past three." means it is:
questions.explanation: "Half past three" = 3:30. "Half past" means 30 minutes after the hour.

questions.question: "Quarter past four" means ___. (4:15 / 4:30 / 4:45)
questions.explanation: "Quarter past" = 15 minutes after the hour. Quarter past four = 4:15.

questions.question: "Quarter to five" means:
questions.explanation: "Quarter to" = 15 minutes before the hour. Quarter to five = 4:45.

questions.question: How many days are in a week?
questions.explanation: There are 7 days in a week: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday.

questions.question: The third month of the year is ___. (March / May / June)
questions.explanation: The months in order: January, February, March... March is the third month.

questions.question: How many months are in a year?
questions.explanation: There are 12 months in a year.

questions.question: "Fortnight" means:
questions.explanation: A fortnight is a British English word meaning two weeks (14 days).

questions.question: The day after Monday is ___. (Tuesday / Wednesday / Sunday)
questions.explanation: Days of the week: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday.

questions.question: How do you say 100?
questions.explanation: 100 = one hundred. We say "one hundred" not just "hundred" in standard English.

questions.question: "Noon" means:
questions.explanation: "Noon" means 12 o'clock midday (12pm).

questions.question: The last month of the year is ___. (November / December / January)
questions.explanation: December is the 12th and last month of the year.

questions.question: How do you write the date "the fifth of June"?
questions.explanation: Both "June 5" (American) and "5th June" (British) are correct ways to write this date.

questions.question: "Decade" means:
questions.explanation: A decade = 10 years. A century = 100 years. A millennium = 1000 years.

questions.question: The day before Friday is ___. (Wednesday / Thursday / Saturday)
questions.explanation: Thursday comes before Friday in the week.

questions.question: How do you say 1,000,000?
questions.explanation: 1,000,000 = one million. 1,000 = one thousand. 1,000,000,000 = one billion.

questions.question: "It's ten to seven." means ___. (6:50 / 7:10 / 6:10)
questions.explanation: "Ten to seven" = 10 minutes before 7:00 = 6:50.

questions.question: Which season comes after winter?
questions.explanation: The four seasons: Spring → Summer → Autumn/Fall → Winter → Spring again.

questions.question: The sky on a clear day is:
questions.explanation: The sky is blue on a clear day.

questions.question: Which color is a mix of red and white?
questions.explanation: Pink is made by mixing red and white together.

questions.question: Grass is ___. (green / yellow / brown)
questions.explanation: Healthy grass is green.

questions.question: "Oval" is a shape that looks like:
questions.explanation: An oval is like a stretched circle — like an egg shape.

questions.question: A shape with three sides is a:
questions.explanation: A triangle has three sides and three angles.

questions.question: The color of the sun is ___. (yellow / orange / white)
questions.explanation: The sun appears yellow (though it is actually white in space).

questions.question: "Transparent" means:
questions.explanation: "Transparent" means you can see through it clearly — like glass or water.

questions.question: Which color is between red and yellow?
questions.explanation: Orange is between red and yellow on the color spectrum.

questions.question: A shape with four equal sides is a ___. (square / rectangle / diamond)
questions.explanation: A square has four equal sides and four right angles.

questions.question: "Enormous" means:
questions.explanation: "Enormous" means extremely large — bigger than just "big" or "large".

questions.question: "Tiny" means:
questions.explanation: "Tiny" means extremely small — even smaller than "small".

questions.question: The color of chocolate is ___. (brown / black / red)
questions.explanation: Chocolate is brown in color.

questions.question: "Rough" is the opposite of:
questions.explanation: "Rough" (textured, not even) is the opposite of "smooth" (even, flat surface).

questions.question: A "pentagon" has how many sides?
questions.explanation: A pentagon has 5 sides. Penta = five in Greek.

questions.question: "Bright red" means the red color is very ___. (dark / light / vivid/strong)
questions.explanation: "Bright" before a color means it is vivid and strong, not pale or dark.

questions.question: "Pale" before a color means:
questions.explanation: "Pale blue" or "pale yellow" means a light, soft version of that color.

questions.question: "Circular" means shaped like a:
questions.explanation: "Circular" means round, shaped like a circle.

questions.question: Purple is made by mixing red and ___. (blue / yellow / green)
questions.explanation: Purple is made by mixing red and blue together.

questions.question: "Dull" when describing color means:
questions.explanation: "Dull" means not bright or vivid — a dull gray, a dull brown.

questions.question: A shape with six sides is called a ___. (hexagon / pentagon / octagon)
questions.explanation: A hexagon has 6 sides. Hexa = six in Greek. Like a honeycomb cell.

questions.question: You are sick and need to see a doctor. What do you say when you call the clinic?
options: I would like to make an appointment, please.
Send a doctor to my house.
questions.explanation: "I would like to make an appointment" is the polite and correct way to book a doctor visit.

questions.question: The doctor asks: "What ___ you here today?" (brings / brought / bring)
questions.explanation: "What brings you here today?" is how doctors ask why you came to see them.

questions.question: You have pain in your stomach. What do you say?
options: I feel stomach pain very much.
questions.explanation: "I have a stomachache" is the correct phrase for stomach pain.

questions.question: The doctor asks "How long have you had this?" What is she asking?
options: How many days have you been sick?
questions.explanation: "How long have you had this?" asks how many days or hours you have had the symptom.

questions.question: You have a high temperature. You say: "I have a ___." (fever / cold / cough)
questions.explanation: A fever means your body temperature is higher than normal — usually above 38°C.

questions.question: The doctor says "Take this twice a day." What does this mean?
options: Take it two times every day.
Take it in the morning only.
questions.explanation: "Twice a day" means two times per day — usually morning and evening.

questions.question: The doctor asks "Are you allergic to any medication?" What is she asking?
options: Do any medicines cause a bad reaction in your body?
questions.explanation: An allergy to medication means a medicine causes a bad reaction like a rash or swelling.

questions.question: You say: "It hurts ___ I breathe deeply." (when / while / if)
questions.explanation: "It hurts when I breathe" tells the doctor the pain happens during a specific action.

questions.question: The doctor says "I am going to refer you to a specialist." What does this mean?
options: She will send you to another doctor who is an expert.
She wants you to go to hospital immediately.
questions.explanation: A referral means your doctor sends you to another doctor who specializes in a particular area.

questions.question: Which phrase correctly describes a sharp sudden pain?
questions.explanation: "Stabbing pain" describes a sharp intense pain. "Dull ache" is a low constant pain. "Mild" means not serious.

questions.question: The nurse says: "Roll up your sleeve — I need to take your ___." (blood / temperature / pulse)
questions.explanation: "Take your blood" means draw blood for a blood test — very common in medical check-ups.

questions.question: The doctor says "You need to fast before the blood test." What does "fast" mean here?
options: Do not eat or drink anything for a period of time.
questions.explanation: To "fast" means to not eat or drink (except water) for several hours before a test.

questions.question: You want to ask if your condition is serious. What do you say?
options: Should I be worried about this?
questions.explanation: "Should I be worried about this?" is the most natural polite way to ask if something is serious.

questions.question: The doctor says: "Get plenty of ___ and drink lots of fluids." (rest / food / exercise)
questions.explanation: "Get plenty of rest" is very common medical advice — sleep and avoid strenuous activity.

questions.question: You feel dizzy. What do you tell the doctor?
options: My head is spinning and I feel unsteady.
questions.explanation: Dizziness is described as feeling like the room is spinning or feeling unsteady on your feet.

questions.question: The doctor says "The results came back negative." What does this usually mean?
options: The results are very bad news.
The test did not find the disease — usually good news.
questions.explanation: In medical tests, "negative" usually means the condition was NOT found — often good news.

questions.question: You tell the nurse: "I am ___ to penicillin." (allergic / sick / sensitive)
questions.explanation: "I am allergic to penicillin" means penicillin causes a bad reaction in your body.

questions.question: The doctor says "Come back for a follow-up in two weeks." What is a follow-up?
options: A second opinion from another doctor
A return visit to check how you are recovering
A phone call to check your progress
questions.explanation: A follow-up appointment is a return visit to check if you are getting better after treatment.

questions.question: You want to know what medicine the doctor prescribed. What do you ask?
questions.explanation: "What is this prescription for?" politely asks what the medicine is meant to treat.

questions.question: You go to the ___ to collect your prescribed medicine. (pharmacy / clinic / hospital)
questions.explanation: A pharmacy (or chemist) is where you collect medicine prescribed by a doctor.

questions.question: You call someone and they answer. How do you start the conversation?
questions.explanation: "Hello, this is [name] calling" is the standard way to introduce yourself on a phone call.

questions.question: You want to speak to someone. You say: "Could I ___ to Mr. Smith, please?" (talk / speak / call)
questions.explanation: "Could I speak to...?" is the correct polite way to ask for someone on the phone.

questions.question: The person you want is not available. What does the receptionist say?
options: I am afraid he is not available at the moment.
He does not want to talk.
questions.explanation: "I am afraid he is not available at the moment" is polite professional language for "he cannot talk now."

questions.question: You want to leave a message. What do you say?
options: Could I leave a message, please?
questions.explanation: "Could I leave a message, please?" is the correct polite phrase for leaving a message.

questions.question: The line is bad. You say: "I am sorry, could you ___ up? I can barely hear you." (speak / talk / hold)
questions.explanation: "Could you speak up?" means please speak louder — the connection or volume is too low.

questions.question: Someone calls you but you are busy. What do you say?
options: I am sorry, could I call you back in a few minutes?
I cannot talk to you now.
This is not a good time, goodbye.
questions.explanation: "Could I call you back?" is the polite way to ask to return the call when you are free.

questions.question: The call is about to end. What is a natural way to say goodbye?
questions.explanation: "Thanks for calling, speak soon!" is a warm and natural way to end a phone conversation.

questions.question: You did not understand what was said. You say: "I am sorry, could you ___ that?" (repeat / say / speak)
questions.explanation: "Could you repeat that?" politely asks someone to say something again.

questions.question: You are calling to confirm an appointment. What do you say?
options: I am calling to confirm my appointment for tomorrow.
questions.explanation: "I am calling to confirm my appointment" is the standard phrase used to check a booking.

questions.question: Someone asks "Is this a good time to talk?" What are they asking?
options: Are you in a good location?
Are you free to have a conversation right now?
Do you have a good phone?
questions.explanation: "Is this a good time to talk?" asks if you are free and available to have a conversation.

questions.question: You want someone to wait on the phone. You say: "Please ___ the line." (hold / keep / stay)
questions.explanation: "Please hold the line" or "hold on" means please wait on the phone while I do something.

questions.question: The receptionist says "I will put you through." What does this mean?
options: She will connect you to the person you want.
questions.explanation: "Put you through" means connect your call to the person or department you need.

questions.question: You call a company but get a recorded message. What is this called?
questions.explanation: An automated message is a pre-recorded message that plays when no one can answer.

questions.question: You want to leave a voicemail. You say: "Hi, this is [name]. Please ___ me back at [number]." (call / phone / ring)
questions.explanation: "Please call me back" is the standard phrase when leaving a voicemail asking for a return call.

questions.question: The connection is bad and keeps cutting out. What do you say?
options: I am sorry, the line is breaking up. Can you hear me?
I will hang up and call again.
questions.explanation: "The line is breaking up" means the connection keeps cutting out and it is hard to hear.

questions.question: You accidentally called the wrong number. What do you say?
options: I am sorry, I think I have the wrong number.
This is not the right person.
questions.explanation: "I think I have the wrong number" is the polite way to apologize for dialing incorrectly.

questions.question: You want to know who is calling. You say: "May I ask who is ___?" (calling / ringing / phoning)
questions.explanation: "May I ask who is calling?" is the polite professional way to ask for the caller's name.

questions.question: Someone says "I will get back to you." What do they mean?
options: They will contact you later with an answer.
They want you to call them.
questions.explanation: "I will get back to you" means I will contact you later with information or an answer.

questions.question: You call someone and they do not answer. You hear a beep and leave a ___. (voicemail / message / note)
questions.explanation: A voicemail is a recorded audio message left when someone does not answer their phone.

questions.question: Someone says "Can I take a message?" What are they offering to do?
options: Write down your name and number and pass it on.
questions.explanation: "Can I take a message?" means they will write down your message and pass it to the right person.

questions.question: You want to suggest going out with a friend. What do you say?
options: Do you want to hang out this weekend?
questions.explanation: "Do you want to hang out?" is a casual and friendly way to suggest spending time together.

questions.question: Your friend asks if you are free. You say: "I am ___ on Saturday. How about Sunday?" (busy / free / tired)
questions.explanation: "I am busy on Saturday" means you are not available — you have something planned.

questions.question: You want to suggest a time to meet. What do you say?
options: When are you free, tell me.
questions.explanation: "How does 3pm sound?" is a natural friendly way to suggest a specific time.

questions.question: Your friend suggests a plan but you cannot make it. What do you say?
options: I would love to but I already have plans. Can we do another day?
questions.explanation: "I would love to but I already have plans" is polite — it shows you want to go but cannot.

questions.question: You agree to your friend's plan. You say: "___ sounds great! I will see you then." (That / This / It)
questions.explanation: "That sounds great!" is a warm enthusiastic way to accept a plan.

questions.question: You want to know where to meet. What do you ask?
questions.explanation: "Where shall we meet?" is a natural question for arranging a meeting point.

questions.question: You are running late. What do you text your friend?
options: Hey, I am running about 10 minutes late — sorry!
questions.explanation: "I am running about 10 minutes late" is polite and gives your friend useful information.

questions.question: You want to confirm the plan. You say: "So we are ___ at the cafe at 4, right?" (meeting / going / coming)
questions.explanation: "We are meeting at the cafe at 4" confirms the time and place of the plan.

questions.question: Your friend cancels at the last minute. What is a polite response?
questions.explanation: "No worries, maybe next time!" is gracious and friendly — it shows you are not upset.

questions.question: You want to suggest an activity. What do you say?
questions.explanation: "Let us watch a movie" or "How about watching a movie?" are natural suggestions.

questions.question: Your friend asks what you want to do. You say: "I am ___ for anything — what do you feel like?" (down / up / open)
questions.explanation: "I am up for anything" means you have no strong preference and are happy to do whatever.

questions.question: You want to make sure your friend has your number. What do you say?
questions.explanation: "Have you got my number?" is a natural British English way to ask if they have your contact number.

questions.question: After a great time with friends, what do you say?
options: That was so much fun — we should do this again soon!
I had a nice time I think.
questions.explanation: "That was so much fun — we should do this again!" is warm and shows you enjoyed yourself.

questions.question: You want to split the bill with friends. You say: "Should we ___?" (split / share / divide)
questions.explanation: "Should we split the bill?" means each person pays their own share.

questions.question: Your friend says "It is on me this time." What does this mean?
questions.explanation: "It is on me" means I will pay for everything this time.

questions.question: You want to invite more people. What do you say?
options: Should we ask anyone else to join us?
questions.explanation: "Should we ask anyone else to join us?" is the natural friendly way to suggest inviting others.

questions.question: You are not sure about your plans yet. You say: "I will have to ___ back to you on that." (get / come / go)
questions.explanation: "I will get back to you" means I will let you know later when I have confirmed my plans.

questions.question: You want to reschedule a cancelled plan. What do you say?
options: Can we try again next week?
questions.explanation: "Can we try again next week?" is positive — you want to reschedule rather than cancel completely.

questions.question: Your friend asks where you want to eat. You say: "I do not mind — you ___." (choose / pick / decide)
questions.explanation: "You pick" or "you choose" means you are happy to let your friend decide.

questions.question: You want to know if a friend enjoyed the evening. What do you ask?
questions.explanation: "Did you have fun?" is the most natural question to ask if someone enjoyed themselves.

questions.question: You received great news. How do you express happiness?
options: I am so thrilled about this!
questions.explanation: "I am so thrilled!" expresses strong happiness and excitement. "Thrilled" means very happy.

questions.question: You feel very tired and have no energy. You say: "I am completely ___." (exhausted / tired / sleepy)
questions.explanation: "Exhausted" means extremely tired — much stronger than just "tired".

questions.question: Something made you very angry. How do you express this?
options: I am absolutely furious about this.
questions.explanation: "Furious" means extremely angry. It is stronger than "angry" or "upset".

questions.question: You are worried about something. What do you say?
options: I am really anxious about my exam tomorrow.
questions.explanation: "Anxious" means worried and nervous about something that might happen.

questions.question: Your friend looks sad. You ask: "Is everything ___?" (okay / good / fine)
questions.explanation: "Is everything okay?" is the most natural way to check if a friend is alright.

questions.question: You feel embarrassed about a mistake. What do you say?
options: I am so embarrassed — my face is red!
questions.explanation: "I am so embarrassed" expresses the feeling of shame or awkwardness in a natural English way.

questions.question: A friend tells you sad news. What is a sympathetic response?
options: I am so sorry to hear that. Are you okay?
questions.explanation: "I am so sorry to hear that" expresses sympathy — it shows you care about their feelings.

questions.question: You feel nervous before a presentation. You say: "I have ___ in my stomach." (butterflies / birds / bees)
questions.explanation: "Butterflies in my stomach" is an idiom meaning you feel nervous or excited about something.

questions.question: Something surprised you greatly. What do you say?
options: I am completely blown away — I had no idea!
questions.explanation: "Blown away" means extremely surprised or impressed — much stronger than just "surprised".

questions.question: You feel calm and content. Which phrase best describes this?
options: I am at peace — everything feels good right now.
questions.explanation: "At peace" describes a calm, content feeling — everything feels balanced and good.

questions.question: You are disappointed with a result. You say: "I am really ___ — I expected better." (disappointed / upset / sad)
questions.explanation: "Disappointed" specifically means you expected something better but it did not happen.

questions.question: Your friend seems upset. What do you say to comfort them?
options: I am here for you — do you want to talk about it?
questions.explanation: "I am here for you" shows support. "Do you want to talk about it?" opens a caring conversation.

questions.question: You feel proud of your achievement. What do you say?
options: I am really proud of myself for this — it took a lot of hard work.
I am happy with the result.
questions.explanation: "I am proud of myself" honestly and naturally expresses the feeling of pride in your achievement.

questions.question: You miss your family very much. You say: "I am really ___ for home." (homesick / missing / longing)
questions.explanation: "Homesick" means you miss your home, family, or country very much when you are away.

questions.question: Something is making you feel uncomfortable. What do you say?
options: I do not like this feeling.
I feel a bit uneasy about this situation.
questions.explanation: "Uneasy" means slightly uncomfortable or unsettled — not quite right but not terrible.

questions.question: You are extremely excited about a trip. What do you say?
options: I am going on a trip.
I cannot wait — I am so excited about this trip!
I look forward to the trip.
questions.explanation: "I cannot wait" and "so excited" together express very strong anticipation and happiness.

questions.question: You feel relieved after finishing a difficult task. You say: "What a ___! I am so glad that is over." (relief / feeling / release)
questions.explanation: "What a relief!" expresses the feeling of relaxation after stress or worry is gone.

questions.question: A friend asks "How are you feeling?" and you are not well emotionally. What is an honest answer?
options: Honestly, I have been feeling a bit down lately.
questions.explanation: "Feeling a bit down" means feeling slightly sad or low — it is honest without being dramatic.

questions.question: You feel very grateful. You say: "I cannot ___ you enough for your help." (thank / praise / appreciate)
questions.explanation: "I cannot thank you enough" means you are extremely grateful — words cannot express how thankful you are.

questions.question: Something made you laugh a lot. What do you say?
options: I was absolutely in stitches — that was hilarious!
questions.explanation: "In stitches" means laughing so much your sides hurt. "Hilarious" means extremely funny.

questions.question: You enter a restaurant. The host asks "How many?" What does this mean?
options: How many people are in your group?
questions.explanation: "How many?" at a restaurant asks how many people are dining together so they can find an appropriate table.

questions.question: You sit down. The waiter asks: "Are you ready to ___?" (order / eat / choose)
questions.explanation: "Are you ready to order?" asks if you have decided what food and drinks you want.

questions.question: You have not decided yet. What do you say to the waiter?
options: Could we have a few more minutes, please?
We are not ready, go away.
questions.explanation: "Could we have a few more minutes?" is the polite way to ask the waiter to give you more time.

questions.question: You want to know what the restaurant recommends. What do you ask?
options: What is the best thing here?
questions.explanation: "What would you recommend?" is the standard phrase to ask for the waiter's personal suggestion.

questions.question: You have a food allergy. You tell the waiter: "I am ___ to nuts." (allergic / sensitive / intolerant)
questions.explanation: "I am allergic to nuts" tells the waiter that nuts cause a dangerous reaction in your body.

questions.question: The food you ordered has not arrived after a long time. What do you say politely?
options: Excuse me, I have been waiting quite a while — could you check on my order?
questions.explanation: "Could you check on my order?" is polite and patient — better than demanding or complaining directly.

questions.question: The waiter asks "How is everything?" What does this mean?
questions.explanation: "How is everything?" is a standard check-in question — the waiter wants to know if you are happy with the food.

questions.question: The food is delicious. You tell the waiter: "This is ___! Please pass our compliments to the chef." (amazing / good / nice)
questions.explanation: "Please pass our compliments to the chef" is a formal way to say the food was excellent.

questions.question: You want more water. What do you say?
options: Could we get some more water, please?
questions.explanation: "Could we get some more water, please?" is the polite way to ask for a refill.

questions.question: You are ready to pay. What do you say to the waiter?
options: Could we have the bill, please?
questions.explanation: "Could we have the bill, please?" is the standard British English request. Americans say "check."

questions.question: The food was not what you expected. You say: "Excuse me, I ordered the chicken but I received the ___." (fish / wrong dish / beef)
questions.explanation: "I ordered X but received the wrong dish" clearly explains the mistake to the waiter.

questions.question: Your friend asks if the food is good. What do you say if it is excellent?
options: It is absolutely delicious — you have to try this!
questions.explanation: "Absolutely delicious" is an enthusiastic positive review. "You have to try this!" invites others to share.

questions.question: You want a dish without a certain ingredient. What do you say?
options: Could I have it without onions, please?
I do not want onions in it.
questions.explanation: "Could I have it without...?" is the most polite way to make a special request about ingredients.

questions.question: You want to know if the dish contains meat. You ask: "Is this ___?" (vegetarian / meat-free / vegan)
questions.explanation: "Is this vegetarian?" asks if the dish contains no meat — important for vegetarians.

questions.question: You want to order a takeaway (to go). What do you say?
options: Could I get this to go, please?
questions.explanation: "Could I get this to go?" (American) or "Could I take this away?" (British) means you want it packaged to eat elsewhere.

questions.question: The service was excellent. What do you say when leaving?
options: Everything was wonderful — thank you so much!
questions.explanation: "Everything was wonderful — thank you so much!" is a warm and appreciative farewell.

questions.question: You want to split the bill equally. You say: "Could we ___ the bill?" (split / divide / share)
questions.explanation: "Could we split the bill?" means each person pays an equal share of the total.

questions.question: The waiter brings the wrong drink. What do you say?
options: Excuse me, I think there may be a mix-up — I ordered orange juice, not lemonade.
questions.explanation: "I think there may be a mix-up" is polite and non-confrontational — it gives the benefit of the doubt.

questions.question: You want to book a table. You call and say: "I would like to make a ___ for two at 7pm." (reservation / booking / table)
questions.explanation: "Make a reservation" means book a table in advance. Both "reservation" and "booking" are correct.

questions.question: The waiter asks "Would you like to see the dessert menu?" What do you say if you are full?
options: No thank you — I am absolutely full, but everything was delicious!
questions.explanation: "I am absolutely full" politely declines dessert while complimenting the meal.

questions.question: The interviewer says "Tell me about yourself." What is the best approach?
options: Talk about your whole life story.
Give a brief summary of your background, skills, and why you want the job.
Say your name and where you are from.
Talk about your hobbies and interests.
questions.explanation: "Tell me about yourself" expects a professional summary — your background, key skills, and career goal.

questions.question: The interviewer asks about your strength. You say: "One of my greatest ___ is my attention to detail." (strengths / skills / qualities)
questions.explanation: "What are your strengths?" is a very common interview question — always give specific examples.

questions.question: The interviewer asks "Why do you want this job?" What is the best answer?
options: I am passionate about this field and I believe my skills are a great match for this role.
My friend told me to apply.
questions.explanation: Showing passion and explaining how your skills match the role is the most impressive answer.

questions.question: The interviewer asks about a weakness. What is the best approach?
options: Mention a real weakness and explain what you are doing to improve it.
Give a fake weakness like "I work too hard."
questions.explanation: Acknowledging a real weakness and showing self-awareness with a plan to improve is always better than fake answers.

questions.question: You want to show enthusiasm. You say: "I am really ___ about this opportunity." (excited / happy / glad)
questions.explanation: "I am really excited about this opportunity" shows genuine enthusiasm for the position.

questions.question: The interviewer asks "Where do you see yourself in 5 years?" What is a good answer?
options: I hope to have your job.
I see myself growing within this company and taking on more responsibility over time.
questions.explanation: Showing ambition to grow within the company while being realistic is the ideal answer.

questions.question: You want to ask about the role. What is a good question to ask at the end?
options: Could you tell me more about the day-to-day responsibilities of this role?
How many days off do I get?
questions.explanation: Asking about day-to-day responsibilities shows genuine interest in the work itself — always impressive.

questions.question: You are asked about teamwork. You say: "I work well ___ others and enjoy collaborative environments." (with / beside / around)
questions.explanation: "I work well with others" is the standard phrase to describe being a good team player.

questions.question: The interviewer asks "Can you describe a challenge you faced at work?" What should you do?
options: Say you have never had challenges.
Use the STAR method — Situation, Task, Action, Result.
questions.explanation: The STAR method (Situation, Task, Action, Result) is the most effective way to answer behavioral questions.

questions.question: The interview is ending. What do you say?
options: Goodbye, hope to hear from you.
Thank you so much for your time — I am very excited about this opportunity.
Let me know when I start.
questions.explanation: Thanking the interviewer and reaffirming your excitement leaves a strong positive final impression.

questions.question: You are asked about your experience. You say: "I have ___ years of experience in customer service." (three / many / some)
questions.explanation: Always give a specific number when asked about experience — vague answers like "some" seem uncertain.

questions.question: The interviewer asks "Are you a team player or do you prefer working alone?" What is the best answer?
options: I enjoy both — I can collaborate effectively in a team but also work independently when needed.
It depends on the situation I suppose.
questions.explanation: Showing flexibility — comfort with both teamwork and independent work — is always the strongest answer.

questions.question: You do not understand a question. What do you say?
options: I am sorry, could you please rephrase that question?
questions.explanation: "Could you please rephrase that?" is polite and shows you want to give an accurate answer.

questions.question: You are asked why you left your last job. You say: "I am looking for new ___ to grow professionally." (challenges / jobs / opportunities)
questions.explanation: "Looking for new opportunities" is a positive, professional way to explain why you are changing jobs.

questions.question: The interviewer says "We will be in touch." What does this mean?
options: They will contact you with a decision.
You did not get the job.
questions.explanation: "We will be in touch" means they will contact you with their decision — it does not confirm either way.

questions.question: You want to follow up after an interview. What do you send?
options: A text message asking if you got the job.
A thank-you email expressing your continued interest.
Nothing — wait for them to contact you.
A phone call the next day.
questions.explanation: A thank-you email within 24 hours is professional and keeps you in the interviewer's mind positively.

questions.question: The interviewer asks about your availability. You say: "I am available to ___ immediately." (start / begin / work)
questions.explanation: "I am available to start immediately" means you can begin working as soon as you are hired.

questions.question: You are asked "What motivates you?" What is the most impressive answer?
options: I am motivated by the challenge of solving problems and seeing the results of my work.
I am motivated when my boss is nice.
questions.explanation: Saying you are motivated by challenge, growth, and results shows professionalism and genuine drive.

questions.question: You describe your communication style: "I am a ___ communicator — I keep things clear and direct." (strong / clear / good)
questions.explanation: "I am a strong communicator" means you communicate effectively, clearly, and confidently.

questions.question: The interviewer asks "Do you have any questions for us?" You should:
options: Ask at least one thoughtful question about the role or company.
questions.explanation: Always ask at least one question — it shows genuine interest. Avoid asking about salary or benefits first.

questions.question: You are at the airport. Where do you go first to check in for your flight?
questions.explanation: The check-in desk is where you register for your flight, drop off luggage, and receive your boarding pass.

questions.question: The airline staff asks: "Did you ___ your bags yourself?" (pack / carry / bring)
questions.explanation: "Did you pack your bags yourself?" is a standard airport security question.

questions.question: You cannot find your gate. What do you ask?
options: Excuse me, could you help me find gate 12?
questions.explanation: "Could you help me find gate 12?" is polite and clear — more natural than just stating the gate number.

questions.question: Your flight is delayed. What does the announcement say?
options: Flight AA101 has been delayed by two hours.
questions.explanation: "Has been delayed by two hours" means the flight will leave two hours later than originally planned.

questions.question: You arrive at a hotel. You say: "I have a ___ under the name Smith." (reservation / booking / room)
questions.explanation: "I have a reservation" is the standard phrase when checking into a hotel.

questions.question: You want a room with a view. What do you ask when booking?
options: Give me a room with windows.
Could I request a room with a sea view, please?
Is there a good room available?
questions.explanation: "Could I request a room with a sea view?" is the polite way to make a special room request.

questions.question: You are at the train station. What do you ask to buy a ticket?
options: Could I have a return ticket to London, please?
I want to go to London.
questions.explanation: "A return ticket" means a ticket for the journey there and back. "Single" means one way only.

questions.question: You ask the train conductor: "Is this seat ___?" (taken / free / empty)
questions.explanation: "Is this seat taken?" asks if someone is already sitting there or has reserved it.

questions.question: You arrive at customs at the airport. The officer asks "What is the purpose of your visit?" What do you say?
options: I am here for tourism — I will be staying for two weeks.
questions.explanation: Being specific — "tourism" and the length of stay — is the expected clear answer at customs.

questions.question: You need to exchange money. Where do you go?
options: A currency exchange office or bank
questions.explanation: Currency exchange offices and banks are the standard places to exchange foreign currency.

questions.question: You want to know the departure time. You ask: "What time does the train ___?" (leave / go / depart)
questions.explanation: "What time does the train depart?" is formal and correct. "Leave" is also perfectly fine.

questions.question: Your luggage has not arrived at baggage claim. What do you do?
options: Go to the airline's lost luggage desk and report it.
questions.explanation: You should report missing luggage to the airline's lost luggage desk immediately.

questions.question: You want to hire a car. What do you say at the car rental desk?
options: I want to borrow a car.
I would like to rent a car for three days, please.
questions.explanation: "I would like to rent a car for [number] days" is the standard phrase at a car rental desk.

questions.question: You are on a plane and want a blanket. You say to the flight attendant: "Could I have a ___ please?" (blanket / pillow / cushion)
questions.explanation: "Could I have a blanket, please?" is a polite request to the cabin crew during a flight.

questions.question: The hotel checkout is at 11am but you want to stay longer. What do you ask?
options: Is it possible to have a late checkout, please?
I want more time in the room.
questions.explanation: "Is it possible to have a late checkout?" is the polite and standard request to extend your room time.

questions.question: You are on a bus and want to get off at the next stop. What do you do?
options: Press the stop button or pull the cord and stand near the door.
Ask another passenger to tell the driver.
questions.explanation: Pressing the stop button signals the driver you want to get off at the next stop — standard on most buses.

questions.question: You ask for directions: "Excuse me, how ___ is the city center from here?" (far / long / much)
questions.explanation: "How far is it?" asks about distance. "How long does it take?" asks about time.

questions.question: You want to upgrade your flight seat. What do you ask?
options: Is it possible to upgrade to business class?
questions.explanation: "Is it possible to upgrade to business class?" is the polite formal way to request a seat upgrade.

questions.question: You are jet-lagged. You tell your friend: "I am completely ___. My body clock is all over the place." (jet-lagged / tired / exhausted)
questions.explanation: "Jet-lagged" means feeling tired and disoriented after a long flight across time zones.

questions.question: You want to know what is included in the hotel price. What do you ask?
options: Is breakfast included in the rate?
questions.explanation: "Is breakfast included in the rate?" is the standard question to ask about hotel inclusions.

questions.question: It is a beautiful sunny day. What do you say to start a conversation?
options: What lovely weather we are having today!
questions.explanation: "What lovely weather we are having!" is a classic British-style small talk opener about nice weather.

questions.question: It is raining heavily. You say: "It is ___ down out there!" (pouring / raining / falling)
questions.explanation: "It is pouring down" or "it is pouring" means it is raining very heavily.

questions.question: Someone asks "Did you catch the game last night?" but you did not watch it. What do you say?
options: I missed it actually — how did it go?
questions.explanation: "I missed it — how did it go?" keeps the conversation going politely even if you did not watch.

questions.question: You want to compliment a colleague's work. What do you say?
options: That presentation was really impressive — great job!
questions.explanation: "That was really impressive — great job!" is warm, specific, and encouraging.

questions.question: It is very cold. You say: "It is absolutely ___ today! Did you feel it this morning?" (freezing / cold / chilly)
questions.explanation: "Freezing" means extremely cold — much stronger than "cold" or "chilly."

questions.question: Someone mentions they just returned from a holiday. What do you ask?
questions.explanation: "Oh lovely! How was it?" is enthusiastic and open — it invites them to share their experience.

questions.question: You pass a colleague in the hallway. What is the most natural thing to say?
questions.explanation: "Hey! How is everything going?" is a casual friendly greeting for someone you know.

questions.question: The weather is very humid. You say: "It is so ___ today — I feel sticky already!" (humid / hot / warm)
questions.explanation: "Humid" means the air has a lot of moisture — it feels hot and sticky.

questions.question: Someone says "Can you believe this weather?" What are they expressing?
options: They are surprised or frustrated by the weather.
They are asking for your opinion on climate.
They want to know if you checked the forecast.
questions.explanation: "Can you believe this weather?" is a rhetorical question expressing surprise or frustration about the weather.

questions.question: You want to end a small talk conversation naturally. What do you say?
options: Anyway, I will let you get on — lovely catching up!
I have to leave now, sorry.
questions.explanation: "I will let you get on" is a very British polite way to end a conversation — it implies they are busy.

questions.question: It is a beautiful day. You say: "What a ___ day — perfect for a walk!" (gorgeous / good / nice)
questions.explanation: "Gorgeous" means extremely beautiful — stronger and more enthusiastic than "nice" or "good."

questions.question: A colleague mentions they are tired. What is a sympathetic response?
options: Oh I know that feeling — it has been a busy week hasn't it?
questions.explanation: Relating to their feeling ("I know that feeling") and creating shared connection is warm and natural.

questions.question: Someone mentions it might snow. What do you say?
options: Oh really? I hope it does — I love snow!
questions.explanation: Sharing your personal feeling about snow keeps the conversation lively and engaging.

questions.question: You see a rainbow after the rain. You say: "Oh look! There is a ___! How beautiful!" (rainbow / arch / light)
questions.explanation: A rainbow appears in the sky after rain when sunlight passes through water droplets.

questions.question: You want to talk about weekend plans as small talk. What do you ask?
options: What are you doing this weekend?
Do you have plans for weekend?
Plans for the weekend, you have?
questions.explanation: "What are you doing this weekend?" is the most natural and grammatically correct question.

questions.question: Someone says the forecast says it will rain all week. How do you respond?
options: Oh no — I was hoping for some sunshine! I suppose I will have to dig out my umbrella.
The weather is always bad here.
questions.explanation: "I was hoping for sunshine" and "dig out my umbrella" make the response personal, relatable, and natural.

questions.question: It is a very windy day. You say: "It is so ___ out there — my umbrella turned inside out!" (windy / breezy / gusty)
questions.explanation: "Windy" is the standard word. "Gusty" means strong sudden bursts of wind. Both are correct.

questions.question: You want to ask what someone does for fun outside of work. What do you say?
options: So what do you get up to outside of work?
questions.explanation: "What do you get up to outside of work?" is a very natural conversational question about someone's personal life.

questions.question: The temperature has dropped a lot. You say: "There has been a real ___ in temperature this week." (drop / fall / change)
questions.explanation: "A drop in temperature" means the temperature has gone down significantly.

questions.question: Someone says "Lovely day, isn't it?" What is the most natural response?
options: Isn't it just! Perfect for being outdoors.
questions.explanation: "Isn't it just!" is a very natural enthusiastic agreement in conversational English. "Perfect for being outdoors" adds warmth.

questions.question: You want to open a bank account. What do you say?
options: I would like to open a current account, please.
How do I get an account?
questions.explanation: "I would like to open a current account" is the correct formal phrase when starting banking.

questions.question: You want to take money out of your account. You say: "I would like to ___ some cash." (withdraw / take / remove)
questions.explanation: "Withdraw" is the correct banking term for taking money out of your account.

questions.question: You want to put money into your account. What do you say?
options: I would like to make a deposit, please.
questions.explanation: "Make a deposit" is the correct term for putting money into a bank account.

questions.question: The bank clerk asks for proof of identity. What document do you show?
questions.explanation: A passport or driving licence is the most accepted form of photo ID at a bank.

questions.question: You want to check how much money you have. You ask: "What is my current ___?" (balance / amount / total)
questions.explanation: "Balance" is the amount of money currently in your account.

questions.question: You need to send money to someone in another country. What do you ask for?
questions.explanation: An "international wire transfer" is the standard term for sending money abroad.

questions.question: The bank says your account is overdrawn. What does this mean?
options: Your account has a lot of money.
You have spent more money than you had in your account.
You need to change your PIN.
questions.explanation: "Overdrawn" means you have spent more than your balance — you owe the bank money.

questions.question: A regular automatic payment from your account is called a ___ order. (standing / regular / direct)
questions.explanation: A "standing order" is a fixed regular automatic payment, like monthly rent.

questions.question: You think someone has used your card without permission. What do you say?
options: I would like to report some suspicious activity on my account.
questions.explanation: "Suspicious activity" is the correct term for unauthorised or unusual transactions on your account.

questions.question: The bank charges a fee for a service. What is a "fee"?
options: Money the bank charges you for a service
questions.explanation: A fee is an amount charged by the bank for providing a service, like international transfers.

questions.question: You want to borrow money from the bank. You apply for a ___. (loan / credit / debt)
questions.explanation: A "loan" is money borrowed from a bank that you pay back with interest over time.

questions.question: The bank offers you an interest rate of 3% on savings. What does this mean?
options: You pay the bank 3% every month.
The bank pays you 3% of your savings each year as a reward.
You can borrow 3% more than your balance.
questions.explanation: Interest on savings means the bank pays you a percentage of your money as a reward for keeping it with them.

questions.question: Your debit card has expired. What do you ask the bank?
options: Could I request a replacement card, please?
questions.explanation: "Could I request a replacement card?" is the polite standard phrase for getting a new card.

questions.question: You forgot your PIN. You ask the bank to ___ it. (reset / change / fix)
questions.explanation: "Reset my PIN" means set up a new PIN number to replace the forgotten one.

questions.question: You receive your monthly bank statement. What is it?
options: A record of all transactions in your account over a period
A receipt for your last payment
questions.explanation: A bank statement is a detailed list of all money in and out of your account over a period, usually monthly.

questions.question: The bank asks you to "verify your identity." What do they want?
options: Proof that you are who you say you are
questions.explanation: "Verify your identity" means the bank needs to confirm who you are, usually with ID documents.

questions.question: Money the bank charges you for borrowing is called ___. (interest / fees / charges)
questions.explanation: Interest is the extra money you pay back on top of a loan — the cost of borrowing.

questions.question: You want your salary paid directly into your account. What is this called?
questions.explanation: "Direct deposit" means your employer sends your salary electronically straight to your bank account.

questions.question: You want to pay a bill automatically each month. You set up a ___ debit. (direct / standing / automatic)
questions.explanation: A "direct debit" allows a company to take variable amounts from your account automatically each month.

questions.question: The bank teller says "Please sign here." Where do you sign?
options: At the top of the form
On the dotted line at the bottom
On the back of your card
questions.explanation: "Please sign here" usually means the designated signature line, typically at the bottom of a document.

questions.question: You want to send a letter. What do you say at the post office?
options: I would like to send this letter, please.
questions.explanation: "I would like to send this letter" is the polite standard phrase at a post office counter.

questions.question: You need to stick something on your envelope to send it. This is called a ___. (stamp / sticker / label)
questions.explanation: A stamp is purchased and stuck on a letter or parcel to pay for postal delivery.

questions.question: The clerk asks "First class or second class?" What does this refer to?
options: The speed and price of delivery
questions.explanation: First class is faster and more expensive; second class is slower and cheaper — both refer to delivery speed.

questions.question: You want your parcel to arrive as quickly as possible. What do you ask for?
questions.explanation: "Express" or "next-day delivery" is the fastest postal option, though more expensive.

questions.question: You want proof that your parcel was received. You ask for ___ delivery. (recorded / tracked / signed)
questions.explanation: "Recorded delivery" or "signed for" means someone must sign when receiving it, giving you proof of delivery.

questions.question: The clerk asks "What is in the parcel?" Why do they ask this?
questions.explanation: Post offices ask about parcel contents for customs declarations on international mail and for safety regulations.

questions.question: You want to send something internationally. What does the clerk ask for?
options: The destination country and a customs declaration form
questions.explanation: International parcels usually require the destination country and a customs form declaring the contents and value.

questions.question: A large item you send by post is called a ___. (parcel / package / both are correct)
questions.explanation: "Parcel" (British English) and "package" (American English) both refer to a wrapped item sent by post.

questions.question: You were not home when a delivery arrived. What do you receive?
options: A missed delivery card or notification
An email from the post office
questions.explanation: A missed delivery card tells you a delivery was attempted and explains how to rearrange or collect it.

questions.question: You want to collect a parcel that is waiting at the post office. What do you say?
options: I am here to collect a parcel — I have the notification card.
questions.explanation: "I am here to collect a parcel" with the notification card is the correct way to collect held mail.

questions.question: The number written on an envelope that helps the postal service find the correct area is called a ___ code. (post / zip / area)
questions.explanation: A postcode (UK) or zip code (US) helps the postal service identify the correct delivery area.

questions.question: How much does it cost to send this parcel? — What is this question asking about?
questions.explanation: "Postage" is the cost of sending something by post — the fee paid for delivery.

questions.question: You want to insure a valuable parcel in case it gets lost. What do you ask?
options: Can I add insurance to this parcel?
questions.explanation: "Add insurance to this parcel" means paying extra so you are compensated if it is lost or damaged.

questions.question: Mail delivered to the wrong address is called ___ delivery. (misdelivered / wrong / incorrect)
questions.explanation: "Misdelivered" means the post was delivered to the wrong address by mistake.

questions.question: The clerk weighs your parcel. Why?
options: To see if it is fragile
Because the postage price depends on the weight
To see if it fits in the post box
questions.explanation: Postage prices are calculated based on weight — heavier parcels cost more to send.

questions.question: You want to redirect your post to a new address. What do you ask?
options: Could I arrange a mail redirect to my new address, please?
questions.explanation: "Mail redirect" is the postal service that forwards your post to a new address when you move.

questions.question: The post office is also used to pay some bills. You go to the ___ to pay. (counter / desk / window)
questions.explanation: The "counter" is the service desk at a post office where transactions are handled.

questions.question: Your parcel has a tracking number. What can you do with it?
options: Use it to open the parcel.
Monitor where your parcel is during delivery online.
Pay for delivery at the post office.
questions.explanation: A tracking number lets you check online exactly where your parcel is during its journey.

questions.question: You receive an unexpected bill in the post. You say: "I received a ___ in the mail today." (bill / letter / invoice)
questions.explanation: A bill is a document requesting payment for a service — utilities, telephone, etc.

questions.question: The post office is closed when you arrive. What do you look for?
options: The opening hours on the door
questions.explanation: Opening hours are usually displayed on the door — useful for planning your next visit.

questions.question: Your manager asks you to complete a task by tomorrow. What do you say?
options: Of course — I will have that ready for you by tomorrow.
questions.explanation: "I will have that ready for you by tomorrow" is professional and reassuring — it confirms the deadline.

questions.question: You do not understand a task. You ask: "Could you ___ that for me, please?" (clarify / explain / repeat)
questions.explanation: "Could you clarify that?" is professional language for asking someone to explain something more clearly.

questions.question: You need more time to finish a project. What do you say to your manager?
options: I wanted to flag that I may need a little more time on this — is that okay?
questions.explanation: "I wanted to flag that I may need more time" is professional — you are being proactive and communicative.

questions.question: A colleague asks for your feedback on their work. What do you say if it is good?
options: This is really well done — I especially liked how you structured the argument.
questions.explanation: Specific praise — mentioning exactly what was good — is more valuable than a vague compliment.

questions.question: You want to share an idea in a meeting. You say: "Could I just ___?" (jump in / speak / say something)
questions.explanation: "Could I jump in?" is a natural phrase to politely interrupt or add to a conversation in a meeting.

questions.question: You made a mistake at work. What do you say to your manager?
options: I want to be transparent — I made an error and here is how I plan to fix it.
I am sorry but it was complicated.
questions.explanation: Taking responsibility and offering a solution is the most professional response to a workplace mistake.

questions.question: Your colleague is talking over you in a meeting. What do you say calmly?
options: If I could just finish my point — thank you.
questions.explanation: "If I could just finish my point" is assertive but polite — professional language for managing interruptions.

questions.question: You want to arrange a meeting. You say: "Could we ___ a time to discuss this?" (find / set / schedule)
questions.explanation: "Could we schedule a time?" is professional language for arranging a meeting.

questions.question: Your manager says "Keep me in the loop." What does this mean?
options: Continue updating them with any developments or progress.
questions.explanation: "Keep me in the loop" means continue telling me what is happening — keep me informed.

questions.question: A task is taking longer than expected. What do you say?
options: This is taking longer than anticipated — I will keep you posted on progress.
I am working on it slowly.
