questions.explanation: "Longer than anticipated" and "keep you posted" are professional phrases showing transparency and communication.

questions.question: You want to work from home one day. You ask: "Would it be okay if I ___ from home tomorrow?" (work / stay / be)
questions.explanation: "Would it be okay if I worked from home?" is the polite way to request remote working.

questions.question: Your colleague is overwhelmed with work. What do you say?
options: Is there anything I can take off your plate? I have some capacity right now.
You should manage your time better.
questions.explanation: "Take something off your plate" means reduce your workload. "I have capacity" means I have spare time to help.

questions.question: The manager says "Let us circle back on this." What does this mean?
options: Return to this topic later after doing something else.
questions.explanation: "Circle back" is common workplace jargon meaning to return to a topic at a later time.

questions.question: You want to give an update on a task. You say: "Just wanted to ___ in on where things stand." (check / loop / touch)
questions.explanation: "Touch base" means make brief contact to check on progress — very common in workplaces.

questions.question: You disagree with a colleague's idea respectfully. What do you say?
options: I see where you are coming from — I just wonder if we could also consider...
I do not agree with that.
questions.explanation: "I see where you are coming from" shows you understand their view before offering a different perspective.

questions.question: Your manager asks for an update. What is a professional response?
options: I am still working on it.
I am about 80% through — I expect to have it completed by end of day.
It is almost done I think.
questions.explanation: Giving a specific percentage and a clear deadline is precise and professional.

questions.question: A colleague says "This is above my pay grade." What do they mean?
options: The task is beyond their responsibility or authority level.
questions.explanation: "Above my pay grade" informally means this decision or task is beyond my level of responsibility.

questions.question: You want to praise a colleague publicly in a meeting. What do you say?
options: I just want to give a shout-out to Sarah — her work on this project was exceptional.
questions.explanation: "Give a shout-out" means publicly acknowledge someone's contribution — warm and professional.

questions.question: The manager says the project is on ___. (track / time / schedule)
questions.explanation: "On track" means the project is progressing as planned and will be completed on time.

questions.question: You want to leave work early today. What do you ask?
options: Would it be alright if I left a little early today? I will make sure everything is in order before I go.
I need to go early today.
questions.explanation: Asking politely and assuring your work is covered is the professional way to request leaving early.

questions.question: You want to view a flat. What do you say to the landlord?
options: I want to see the flat.
I would like to arrange a viewing, please.
questions.explanation: "Arrange a viewing" is the standard phrase for booking an appointment to see a property.

questions.question: The person who owns the property you rent is called your ___. (landlord / owner / manager)
questions.explanation: A landlord is the person or company that owns a property and rents it to tenants.

questions.question: You are asked to pay a deposit before moving in. What is a deposit?
options: A sum of money held as security against damage, returned when you leave
A fee to the estate agent
questions.explanation: A deposit is held by the landlord and returned at the end of your tenancy if no damage is caused.

questions.question: The boiler is broken and there is no hot water. What do you say to your landlord?
options: I wanted to report a maintenance issue — the boiler has stopped working.
The boiler is broken, come now.
questions.explanation: "Report a maintenance issue" is the professional phrase for telling your landlord about a repair needed.

questions.question: The legal agreement between you and your landlord is called a ___ agreement. (tenancy / rental / lease)
questions.explanation: A tenancy agreement (or lease) is the legal contract between the tenant and landlord.

questions.question: Your landlord wants to visit the flat. What right do you have?
options: They can come any time they like.
They must give you reasonable notice — usually at least 24 hours.
They cannot visit without your written permission.
They must visit only on weekdays.
questions.explanation: In most countries, landlords must give reasonable notice — usually 24 hours — before entering a tenanted property.

questions.question: You want to know if pets are allowed. What do you ask?
options: Can I have a dog here?
Do you allow pets in flats?
questions.explanation: "Is the property pet-friendly?" is the standard polite question about whether pets are permitted.

questions.question: The money you pay every month to live somewhere is called ___. (rent / fee / payment)
questions.explanation: "Rent" is the regular payment made to a landlord for the right to live in their property.

questions.question: Bills like gas, electricity, and water are called:
questions.explanation: "Utilities" is the collective term for essential services like gas, electricity, and water.

questions.question: You are moving out. What should you do to protect your deposit?
options: Just clean the flat and leave.
Take photos of the entire property and document its condition on the day you leave.
Ask the landlord to inspect it first.
questions.explanation: Photographic evidence of the property condition on move-out day protects you from unfair deposit deductions.

questions.question: You want to extend your tenancy. You ask your landlord: "Could we ___ the tenancy for another six months?" (renew / extend / continue)
questions.explanation: "Renew the tenancy" means start a new tenancy agreement after the current one ends.

questions.question: The flat is described as "furnished." What does this mean?
questions.explanation: "Furnished" means the property comes with furniture like beds, sofas, and tables already included.

questions.question: You want to know the total monthly cost. What do you ask?
options: What is the monthly rent, and are bills included?
How much do I pay you?
What is the cost per month total?
questions.explanation: Asking specifically about rent and whether bills are included gives you a clear picture of total monthly costs.

questions.question: A property without any furniture is described as ___. (unfurnished / empty / bare)
questions.explanation: "Unfurnished" means the property has no furniture — you must provide your own.

questions.question: The landlord is trying to keep your deposit unfairly. What can you do?
options: Contact a tenancy deposit scheme or housing authority to dispute it.
Take the landlord to court immediately.
questions.explanation: Most countries have tenancy deposit schemes or housing authorities that help resolve deposit disputes fairly.

questions.question: What does "notice period" mean in a tenancy?
options: The time the landlord takes to respond to repairs.
The time you must give before ending the tenancy.
The time before you can move in.
questions.explanation: A notice period is the amount of advance warning required — usually one month — before ending a tenancy.

questions.question: You complain about noisy neighbours. You say: "There is a noise ___." (complaint / issue / problem)
questions.explanation: "Noise complaint" is the standard term for reporting disturbance from neighbours.

questions.question: You want to hang pictures on the walls. What do you ask your landlord?
options: Is it okay to put some picture hooks in the walls?
questions.explanation: "Is it okay to put picture hooks in the walls?" is specific and polite — some landlords prohibit wall damage.

questions.question: The flat has damp walls. You tell the landlord: "There is a ___ problem in the bedroom." (damp / wet / moisture)
questions.explanation: "Damp" refers to unwanted moisture in walls or ceilings — a common and serious housing issue.

questions.question: Your landlord increases your rent. What is your right?
options: Check whether proper notice was given and whether the increase is within legal limits.
questions.explanation: Rent increases must usually follow legal rules about notice periods and maximum increases — always check your rights.

questions.question: There is a fire. What do you shout?
questions.explanation: "Fire! Everyone get out!" is the immediate alert — warn others and evacuate immediately.

questions.question: Someone has collapsed. You call emergency services and say: "I need an ___!" (ambulance / doctor / nurse)
questions.explanation: "I need an ambulance" is the first thing to say when someone needs urgent medical help.

questions.question: You witness a car accident. What is the first thing you do?
options: Call 999 or 911 and describe the situation clearly.
Wait for someone else to call.
questions.explanation: Calling emergency services first is always the priority — trained professionals need to be on their way.

questions.question: The operator asks "What is your emergency?" What do you say?
options: There has been an accident — I am at [location] and someone is injured.
questions.explanation: State the type of emergency and your exact location clearly — this is the most critical information.

questions.question: You think someone is having a heart attack. You tell the operator: "He is having chest ___ and cannot breathe." (pain / ache / pressure)
questions.explanation: "Chest pain" is the key phrase for describing a potential heart attack to emergency services.

questions.question: Your bag has been stolen. What do you say to the police?
options: I would like to report a theft — my bag was stolen about ten minutes ago.
questions.explanation: "I would like to report a theft" is the formal correct phrase when reporting a crime to police.

questions.question: You are lost and it is getting dark. What do you say to a stranger?
options: Excuse me, I am sorry to bother you — I am completely lost. Could you help me?
questions.explanation: Polite, clear communication with a stranger in an emergency increases the chance of getting help.

questions.question: Someone is choking. You shout: "Is anyone here trained in ___?" (first aid / medicine / CPR)
questions.explanation: "First aid" covers emergency techniques including the Heimlich manoeuvre for choking.

questions.question: There is a gas smell in your home. What do you do?
options: Open windows, leave immediately, and call the gas emergency line.
Wait to see if it gets stronger.
questions.explanation: A gas smell is a serious emergency — never use flames or switches. Leave and call for help immediately.

questions.question: You find someone unconscious. What do you check first?
questions.explanation: Check if the person is breathing — this determines whether CPR is needed immediately.

questions.question: You need to tell the operator your location. You say: "I am at ___ Street, near the pharmacy." (the exact number + street name)
questions.explanation: Always give your exact address including number, street, and a nearby landmark when calling emergency services.

questions.question: Your child has swallowed something dangerous. What do you call?
options: Poison control or emergency services immediately
questions.explanation: Poison control centres and emergency services are the immediate contacts when a child swallows something dangerous.

questions.question: The operator tells you to "stay on the line." What does this mean?
options: Do not hang up — keep the phone connection open.
questions.explanation: "Stay on the line" means keep the phone call open so the operator can continue helping and monitoring.

questions.question: You hurt your ankle badly and cannot walk. You ask a bystander: "Please ___ an ambulance for me." (call / get / bring)
questions.explanation: "Please call an ambulance" is the clear, direct request for emergency medical help.

questions.question: There is a power cut in your building. What do you do?
options: Contact your electricity provider and report the outage.
Wait for it to come back.
questions.explanation: Report power outages to your electricity provider — they track outages and restore service.

questions.question: Someone is threatening you in the street. What do you do?
options: Move to a public area, call 999/911, and try not to be alone.
Give them what they want immediately.
questions.explanation: Getting to a public area and calling emergency services is the safest response to being threatened.

questions.question: You witness a crime. You tell the police: "I would like to give a ___." (statement / report / description)
questions.explanation: A "witness statement" is your official account of what you saw — important for police investigations.

questions.question: You are in a foreign country and need help. What do you look for?
questions.explanation: Your country's embassy or consulate can help citizens in serious emergencies abroad — lost passports, arrests, etc.

questions.question: You have been in an accident and are hurt. You tell the paramedic: "I have a ___ pain in my back." (sharp / bad / shooting)
questions.explanation: "Sharp pain" describes an intense, sudden, knife-like pain — important medical information for paramedics.

questions.question: The fire alarm goes off. What should you do?
options: Ignore it if you think it is a test.
Evacuate immediately using the stairs and go to the assembly point.
Use the lift to get out faster.
Wait for someone to tell you it is real.
questions.explanation: Always evacuate when a fire alarm sounds — use stairs not lifts, and go to the designated assembly point.

questions.question: You arrive late to a meeting. What do you say?
options: I sincerely apologise for being late — thank you for your patience.
questions.explanation: "I sincerely apologise" is formal and shows genuine regret — stronger than a simple "sorry."

questions.question: You say: "I take full ___ for this mistake." (responsibility / blame / fault)
questions.explanation: "I take full responsibility" is the professional and mature way to acknowledge that a mistake was yours.

questions.question: Someone is upset with you. What is the best first response?
options: I understand why you feel that way — let me explain what happened.
questions.explanation: Acknowledging the other person's feelings before explaining yourself shows emotional intelligence.

questions.question: You want to resolve a dispute calmly. What do you say?
options: Could we find a moment to talk through this properly? I would like to resolve it.
You need to listen to me.
This needs to be sorted now.
questions.explanation: "Could we find a moment to talk through this?" is calm, constructive, and solution-focused.

questions.question: You say: "That was not my ___ — I can see how it upset you and I am sorry." (intention / plan / fault)
questions.explanation: "That was not my intention" means you did not mean to cause the problem — but you still acknowledge the impact.

questions.question: A customer is angry about your service. What do you say?
options: I am truly sorry for the experience you have had — let me see what I can do to put this right.
questions.explanation: "Let me see what I can do to put this right" is customer service language — apologetic and solution-focused.

questions.question: Someone refuses to accept your apology. What do you do?
options: Argue that they should forgive you.
Respect their feelings and give them space — say you hope to repair things when they are ready.
Walk away and forget about it.
questions.explanation: Forcing forgiveness is counterproductive. Giving space while expressing hope to reconcile is respectful.

questions.question: You say: "I should have ___ before acting." (thought / asked / checked)
questions.explanation: "I should have checked first" acknowledges that you should have verified something before proceeding.

questions.question: You want to offer a solution after apologising. What do you say?
options: To make up for this, I would like to offer you a full refund.
I hope this does not happen again.
What do you want me to do?
questions.explanation: Offering a concrete solution after an apology shows you are committed to making things right.

questions.question: You misunderstood someone. What do you say?
options: You did not explain it well.
I apologise — I completely misunderstood what you meant. Could you explain again?
questions.explanation: Taking ownership of the misunderstanding ("I misunderstood") is more respectful than blaming the other person.

questions.question: You say: "I ___ that I handled that situation poorly." (acknowledge / admit / accept)
questions.explanation: "I acknowledge that I handled this poorly" is a mature, professional admission without excessive self-criticism.

questions.question: There was a billing error. You contact the company. What do you say?
options: I believe there may be an error on my invoice — could you help me look into this?
questions.explanation: "I believe there may be an error" is polite and gives the benefit of the doubt — more effective than accusation.

questions.question: You want to prevent a problem from happening again. What do you say?
options: I have put measures in place to make sure this does not happen again.
questions.explanation: "I have put measures in place" shows specific action — more convincing than simply promising it will not recur.

questions.question: You say: "I ___ your frustration and I want to make this right." (understand / feel / hear)
questions.explanation: "I understand your frustration" validates the other person's feelings — essential in conflict resolution.

questions.question: A colleague did something that annoyed you. How do you raise it?
options: Could we have a quick word in private? There is something I wanted to raise with you.
Ignore it and hope it stops.
questions.explanation: "Could we have a quick word in private?" is the most professional and respectful way to address an issue.

questions.question: Someone apologises to you. What is a gracious response?
options: Thank you for saying that — I appreciate it. Let us move forward.
I accept your apology I suppose.
questions.explanation: "Let us move forward" after accepting an apology is positive and constructive for the relationship.

questions.question: You say: "I ___ for any inconvenience this may have caused." (apologise / regret / am sorry)
questions.explanation: "I apologise for any inconvenience" is standard formal language in written apologies and customer service.

questions.question: You want to check if someone is satisfied after resolving an issue. What do you say?
options: I hope this has resolved the issue for you — please let me know if there is anything else I can do.
questions.explanation: Checking satisfaction and offering further help is professional customer service and relationship management.

questions.question: You say: "I ___ should have communicated this sooner." (probably / definitely / clearly)
questions.explanation: "I definitely should have communicated this sooner" is honest and shows self-awareness.

questions.question: You receive a formal complaint. What is the professional first step?
options: Dismiss it if you think it is wrong.
Acknowledge receipt of the complaint and confirm you will investigate promptly.
Ask who else knows about it.
questions.explanation: Acknowledging receipt and confirming investigation is the professional first response to any formal complaint.

questions.question: Your friend just passed a difficult exam. What do you say?
options: That is brilliant — you must be so proud of yourself!
questions.explanation: "That is brilliant" and "you must be so proud" are warm and enthusiastic compliments that celebrate the achievement.

questions.question: You like someone's outfit. You say: "You look absolutely ___!" (stunning / nice / good)
questions.explanation: "You look stunning" is a strong compliment meaning extremely beautiful or impressive in appearance.

questions.question: A friend is nervous before a presentation. How do you encourage them?
options: You are going to be amazing — I have every confidence in you!
questions.explanation: "I have every confidence in you" is a powerful, sincere encouragement that builds the person's self-belief.

questions.question: Someone cooked a delicious meal. What do you say?
options: This is absolutely incredible — you are such a talented cook!
questions.explanation: "Absolutely incredible" combined with a specific compliment about their skill makes the praise much more meaningful.

questions.question: You tell a friend: "You handled that situation with such ___." (grace / skill / calm)
questions.explanation: "You handled that with such grace" means you managed a difficult situation in a dignified and elegant way.

questions.question: Someone receives a compliment. What is a natural modest response?
options: I know, I am very good.
That is so kind of you to say — thank you!
questions.explanation: "That is so kind of you to say" is warm and gracious — it accepts the compliment without false modesty or arrogance.

questions.question: A colleague does excellent work. What do you say?
options: I just wanted to say your work on this has been outstanding — it really shows.
Well done for doing your job.
questions.explanation: "Outstanding" and "it really shows" make the compliment specific and genuine rather than generic.

questions.question: You say: "I am really ___ of how far you have come." (proud / happy / glad)
questions.explanation: "I am really proud of how far you have come" expresses admiration for someone's growth and progress.

questions.question: A friend is going through something difficult. What do you say to encourage them?
options: It will be fine, do not worry.
You are stronger than you think — I have seen how resilient you are.
Keep going, you can do it.
questions.explanation: "You are stronger than you think" and "I have seen how resilient you are" is specific and deeply encouraging.

questions.question: Someone shares creative work with you. What is an encouraging response?
options: This is genuinely impressive — you have a real talent for this.
questions.explanation: "Genuinely impressive" and "real talent" are specific and sincere — more powerful than vague approval.

questions.question: You say: "The way you spoke up in that meeting was really ___." (brave / good / impressive)
questions.explanation: "That was really brave" acknowledges when someone did something that took courage — very meaningful.

questions.question: Your friend just started a new venture. What do you say?
options: I think this is such an exciting step — I am rooting for you all the way!
questions.explanation: "I am rooting for you" is a warm expression of support — it means you are hoping and cheering for their success.

questions.question: Someone gives an excellent explanation. What do you say?
options: That was such a clear and insightful explanation — you made it really easy to understand.
questions.explanation: Specific praise — "clear and insightful" and "easy to understand" — is far more meaningful than a generic "good."

questions.question: You tell someone: "You should be really ___ of what you have achieved." (proud / happy / pleased)
questions.explanation: "You should be really proud of what you have achieved" is a meaningful, heartfelt compliment.

questions.question: Someone helped you enormously. What do you say?
options: I honestly do not know what I would have done without you — thank you so much.
questions.explanation: "I honestly do not know what I would have done without you" expresses deep, genuine gratitude.

questions.question: You want to compliment someone's character. What do you say?
options: You are one of the most genuinely kind people I know — it really says a lot about who you are.
questions.explanation: Complimenting someone's character specifically and sincerely is one of the most meaningful things you can say.

questions.question: You say: "I really ___ your honesty in that situation." (admire / respect / appreciate)
questions.explanation: "I admire your honesty" expresses genuine respect for someone's integrity and courage to be truthful.

questions.question: A student is struggling. How do you encourage them?
options: Do not give up — every expert was once a beginner. You are making progress every day.
This is supposed to be hard.
Keep trying, you will get it.
questions.explanation: "Every expert was once a beginner" is a meaningful, reframing encouragement that reduces shame and builds hope.

questions.question: You say: "Your dedication to this is truly ___." (inspiring / impressive / amazing)
questions.explanation: "Truly inspiring" means the person's dedication motivates others — one of the highest compliments.

questions.question: Someone shares difficult news but is handling it well. What do you say?
options: The strength you are showing right now is incredible — I really admire you.
It is good that you are okay.
questions.explanation: "The strength you are showing is incredible" is deeply meaningful and acknowledges the courage it takes to cope.

questions.question: Which body part pumps blood around your body?
questions.explanation: The heart is the muscle that pumps blood continuously around the entire body.

questions.question: You breathe with your ___. (lungs / kidneys / liver)
questions.explanation: The lungs take in oxygen and release carbon dioxide — essential for breathing.

questions.question: The organ that filters waste from your blood and makes urine is the:
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter waste products from the blood and produce urine.

questions.question: "Sprain" an ankle means:
options: Stretch or tear the ligaments around the ankle
questions.explanation: A sprained ankle means the ligaments — the bands connecting bones — have been overstretched or torn.

questions.question: The large organ in your abdomen that processes food and filters toxins is the ___. (liver / stomach / intestine)
questions.explanation: The liver processes nutrients from food, filters toxins, and produces bile for digestion.

questions.question: Which part of the body do you use to grip and hold things?
questions.explanation: The palm and fingers are used to grip, hold, and manipulate objects.

questions.question: "Chronic" illness means:
options: A sudden illness that lasts one week
A long-lasting illness that continues over a long period
An illness that cannot be treated
questions.explanation: "Chronic" means lasting a long time — often months or years — as opposed to "acute" which is short-term.

questions.question: The tube connecting your mouth to your stomach is called the ___. (oesophagus / windpipe / intestine)
questions.explanation: The oesophagus (esophagus in US English) is the tube food travels down from the throat to the stomach.

questions.question: "Nauseous" means:
questions.explanation: "Nauseous" means feeling sick and like you might vomit — an important symptom to describe to a doctor.

questions.question: The joint connecting your upper and lower arm is the:
questions.explanation: The elbow is the joint in the middle of the arm that allows bending movement.

questions.question: The organ that digests food using acid and enzymes is the ___. (stomach / liver / intestine)
questions.explanation: The stomach uses acid and digestive enzymes to break down food after swallowing.

questions.question: "Inflammation" means:
options: Redness, swelling, and pain in part of the body
questions.explanation: Inflammation is the body's response to injury or infection — causing redness, swelling, heat, and pain.

questions.question: Your "pulse" refers to:
options: The rhythmic beating of your heart felt in blood vessels
questions.explanation: Your pulse is the rhythmic throbbing felt in arteries as the heart pumps — usually measured at the wrist.

questions.question: The skin covers your ___. (whole body / muscles / bones)
questions.explanation: The skin is the body's largest organ and covers the entire external surface of the body.

questions.question: "Immune system" refers to:
options: The body's defence system against infections and disease
questions.explanation: The immune system is the body's natural defence against bacteria, viruses, and other harmful substances.

questions.question: The organ at the back of the skull that controls balance and coordination is the:
questions.explanation: The cerebellum coordinates movement, balance, and fine motor control.

questions.question: Bones are connected to muscles by ___. (tendons / ligaments / cartilage)
questions.explanation: Tendons connect muscles to bones. Ligaments connect bones to other bones.

questions.question: "Fracture" means:
questions.explanation: A fracture is a broken or cracked bone — ranging from hairline fractures to complete breaks.

questions.question: The soft flexible tissue covering the ends of bones in joints is called ___. (cartilage / ligament / tendon)
questions.explanation: Cartilage is the smooth, flexible tissue that cushions and protects bones where they meet in joints.

questions.question: "Blood pressure" measures:
options: The force of blood pushing against artery walls
The amount of blood in your body
questions.explanation: Blood pressure measures the force of blood against artery walls — high blood pressure is a serious health risk.

questions.question: A garment you wear on your upper body with buttons down the front is a:
questions.explanation: A shirt typically has buttons down the front — either casual or formal.

questions.question: You wear these on your feet inside your shoes. They are called ___. (socks / stockings / tights)
questions.explanation: Socks are worn on feet inside shoes to provide comfort and absorb moisture.

questions.question: "Smart casual" dress code means:
options: Neat and stylish but not fully formal
questions.explanation: "Smart casual" is between formal and relaxed — clean, well-fitted clothes but not a formal suit.

questions.question: A garment worn over clothes to keep warm, with long sleeves and a zip or buttons, is a:
questions.explanation: A jacket is an outer garment worn over clothes for warmth or style — shorter than a coat.

questions.question: Clothes that need to be washed are called ___ laundry. (dirty / soiled / used)
questions.explanation: Dirty laundry or "laundry" generally refers to clothes that need washing.

questions.question: "Tailored" clothing means:
options: Clothes made or adjusted to fit your body precisely
questions.explanation: Tailored clothing is made or altered to fit an individual's body measurements precisely.

questions.question: A long formal garment worn by women at weddings or formal events is a:
questions.explanation: A gown is a long, formal dress often worn to weddings, galas, or formal evening events.

questions.question: The number describing your clothing size is called your ___. (size / measurement / fit)
questions.explanation: "Size" refers to the standardised number (like 10, 12, 14 or S, M, L, XL) used for clothing.

questions.question: "Accessories" in fashion refers to:
options: Items like bags, jewellery, belts, and scarves that complement an outfit
questions.explanation: Accessories are items worn in addition to clothing — bags, jewellery, scarves, hats, belts, and watches.

questions.question: Clothes described as "wrinkled" or "creased" need to be:
questions.explanation: "Wrinkled" or "creased" clothes have folds and lines from storage — ironing removes these.

questions.question: A knitted warm top without buttons that you pull over your head is called a ___. (jumper / cardigan / jacket)
questions.explanation: A jumper (UK) or sweater (US) is a knitted top pulled over the head — no buttons or zip.

questions.question: "Business attire" means:
options: Casual clothes for working from home
Formal professional clothing like suits and smart dresses
questions.explanation: "Business attire" means formal professional clothing — suits, blazers, smart trousers, and professional dresses.

questions.question: The part of a shoe that touches the ground is the:
questions.explanation: The sole is the bottom part of the shoe that makes contact with the ground.

questions.question: Clothes that have gone out of fashion are called ___. (dated / old / outdated)
questions.explanation: "Dated" clothes are those that look old-fashioned — they were fashionable in the past but no longer are.

questions.question: "Second-hand" clothing means:
options: Clothing previously owned and worn by someone else
questions.explanation: "Second-hand" means the clothing was previously owned and used by someone else — often sold in charity shops.

questions.question: A "collar" on a shirt refers to:
options: The fold of fabric around the neck
questions.explanation: The collar is the folded or standing strip of fabric around the neckline of a shirt or jacket.

questions.question: Clothes specifically designed to be worn for exercise or sport are called ___wear. (sports / active / athletic)
questions.explanation: "Sportswear" or "activewear" refers to clothing designed for exercise, sport, and physical activity.

questions.question: "Linen" is a type of:
options: Natural fabric made from flax plant fibres
Heavy fabric used for winter coats
questions.explanation: Linen is a natural fabric made from flax fibres — breathable and lightweight, popular in warm climates.

questions.question: The line where two pieces of fabric are sewn together is called a ___. (seam / hem / stitch)
questions.explanation: A seam is where two pieces of fabric are joined by sewing — visible as a line on the inside of garments.

questions.question: "Compliment" someone's outfit means:
options: Say something positive about their clothes
Ask where they bought their clothes
questions.explanation: To "compliment" someone's outfit means to say something kind and positive about how they look.

questions.question: A person who designs buildings is called an:
questions.explanation: An architect designs buildings and structures — creating plans and overseeing construction.

questions.question: A person who defends people in court or gives legal advice is called a ___. (lawyer / judge / solicitor)
questions.explanation: A lawyer (or solicitor/barrister in UK English) provides legal advice and represents people in legal matters.

questions.question: A "pharmacist" works in a:
questions.explanation: A pharmacist works in a pharmacy, dispensing prescribed medicines and advising on medications.

questions.question: An "accountant" primarily deals with:
questions.explanation: An accountant manages financial records, prepares tax returns, and audits financial statements.

questions.question: A person who fixes electrical systems in buildings is called an ___. (electrician / plumber / engineer)
questions.explanation: An electrician installs, maintains, and repairs electrical wiring and systems in buildings.

questions.question: A "consultant" is someone who:
options: Provides expert advice in a particular field
questions.explanation: A consultant is an expert who advises organisations — in areas like management, IT, finance, or strategy.

questions.question: A person who studies and forecasts the weather is a:
questions.explanation: A meteorologist studies the atmosphere and weather patterns to forecast weather conditions.

questions.question: A person who flies an aircraft is called a ___. (pilot / navigator / captain)
questions.explanation: A pilot is trained and licensed to fly aircraft — both the captain and first officer are pilots.

questions.question: A "civil servant" works for:
questions.explanation: A civil servant is employed by the government to implement public policy and deliver government services.

questions.question: An "entrepreneur" is someone who:
options: Starts and runs their own business, taking financial risks
questions.explanation: An entrepreneur creates and runs their own business ventures, taking on financial risk for potential gain.

questions.question: A person who writes articles for newspapers or online is a ___. (journalist / writer / editor)
questions.explanation: A journalist researches, writes, and reports news stories for newspapers, magazines, or digital media.

questions.question: A "surgeon" is a doctor who:
questions.explanation: A surgeon is a specialist medical doctor who performs operations (surgery) on patients.

questions.question: "Self-employed" means:
options: Working for a large company on a contract
Working for yourself, running your own business
Being unemployed but looking for work
questions.explanation: Being self-employed means you work for yourself — not an employee of someone else's business.

questions.question: A person who installs and repairs water pipes is called a ___. (plumber / builder / electrician)
questions.explanation: A plumber installs, maintains, and repairs pipes, water systems, and sanitary equipment.

questions.question: A "social worker" helps:
options: Vulnerable individuals and families access support and services
questions.explanation: Social workers support vulnerable people — including children, elderly people, and those in crisis — to access help.

questions.question: "Freelance" work means:
options: Working independently for multiple clients on different projects
questions.explanation: Freelance work means being self-employed and offering services to multiple clients rather than having one employer.

questions.question: A person who translates spoken language between two people in real time is called an ___. (interpreter / translator / linguist)
questions.explanation: An interpreter translates spoken language in real time. A translator works with written texts.

questions.question: A "recruiter" is responsible for:
options: Finding and hiring suitable candidates for job vacancies
questions.explanation: A recruiter finds, assesses, and hires candidates to fill job vacancies within an organisation.

questions.question: A person who manages the finances and accounts of a company is called a ___. (treasurer / accountant / finance manager)
questions.explanation: A treasurer manages an organisation's finances — though accountant and finance manager are also correct in different contexts.

questions.question: "Redundancy" in employment means:
options: Being promoted to a higher role
Losing your job because the position is no longer needed
Being moved to a different department
questions.explanation: Redundancy means your job role is no longer needed by the company — you lose your job through no fault of your own.

questions.question: The room where you cook food is called the:
questions.explanation: The kitchen is the room specifically designed for cooking and preparing food.

questions.question: The piece of furniture you sleep on is called a ___. (bed / cot / mattress)
questions.explanation: A bed is the main piece of sleeping furniture — consisting of a frame and mattress.

questions.question: A "hallway" in a home refers to:
options: The corridor or passage connecting rooms
The entrance porch outside the front door
The landing at the top of the stairs
questions.explanation: A hallway is the corridor or passage inside a home that connects different rooms.

questions.question: A "chest of drawers" is used for:
questions.explanation: A chest of drawers has several pull-out compartments (drawers) used for storing folded clothes.

questions.question: The room where you eat meals with your family is the ___ room. (dining / sitting / living)
questions.explanation: The dining room is specifically used for eating meals, usually containing a table and chairs.

questions.question: "Utility room" refers to:
options: A room for laundry, cleaning equipment, and household tasks
questions.explanation: A utility room contains the washing machine, dryer, and is used for household chores and cleaning.

questions.question: The piece of furniture you sit on to watch TV or relax, typically with a back and arms, is a:
questions.explanation: A sofa (or couch) is an upholstered seat for multiple people, with a back and armrests — central to living rooms.

questions.question: The material used to make walls look neat and even before painting is called ___. (plaster / paint / filler)
questions.explanation: Plaster is applied to walls and ceilings to create a smooth, even surface before decorating.

questions.question: "Insulation" in a home refers to:
options: Material that prevents heat from escaping, keeping the home warm
questions.explanation: Insulation is material fitted in walls, roofs, and floors to prevent heat loss and reduce energy bills.

questions.question: A "skylight" is:
options: A window built into the roof or ceiling
A glass door to the garden
questions.explanation: A skylight is a window installed in a roof or ceiling to let natural light into a room below.

questions.question: The system that heats your home using hot water through pipes and radiators is called central ___. (heating / warming / boiling)
questions.explanation: Central heating uses a boiler to heat water which flows through pipes to radiators throughout the home.

questions.question: "Mortgage" refers to:
options: Monthly rent paid to a landlord
A long-term loan from a bank to buy a property
questions.explanation: A mortgage is a long-term bank loan used to purchase a property, repaid over many years with interest.

questions.question: The wooden or plastic covering around the bottom of interior walls is called:
questions.explanation: Skirting boards run along the bottom of interior walls, covering the join between wall and floor.

questions.question: The system that removes waste water from sinks, baths, and toilets is called ___. (drainage / plumbing / sewage)
questions.explanation: Plumbing refers to the system of pipes that supplies water and removes waste water in a building.

questions.question: "Renovate" a home means to:
options: Make major improvements or repairs to an existing property
questions.explanation: To renovate means to substantially improve, repair, or modernise a property — larger than just decorating.

questions.question: A "conservatory" is:
options: A glass-roofed room attached to the main house
questions.explanation: A conservatory is a glass-roofed and glass-walled room attached to the house — popular in UK homes.

questions.question: The area of land around and belonging to a house is called the ___. (garden / yard / grounds)
questions.explanation: "Garden" (UK) or "yard" (US) refers to the outdoor land area around a house.

questions.question: "Damp proof course" in a house protects against:
options: Moisture rising from the ground through walls
questions.explanation: A damp proof course is a barrier in walls that prevents groundwater from rising up and causing damp problems.

questions.question: The room or area at the very top of a house under the roof is called the ___. (attic / loft / both are correct)
questions.explanation: "Attic" (American English) and "loft" (British English) both refer to the storage space under the roof.

questions.question: A "ground floor" in British English refers to:
options: The floor at street level — the first floor you enter
The first floor above street level
The top floor of a building
questions.explanation: In British English, the ground floor is at street level. The next floor up is the first floor. Americans call the ground floor "the first floor."

questions.question: A sudden heavy rain that does not last long is called a:
questions.explanation: A shower is a brief period of rainfall — heavier than drizzle but shorter than prolonged rain.

questions.question: A very strong wind storm with heavy rain is called a ___. (hurricane / typhoon / cyclone — all are correct depending on region)
questions.explanation: The same weather system is called a hurricane (Atlantic), typhoon (Pacific), or cyclone (Indian Ocean).

questions.question: "Drought" refers to:
options: A long period with little or no rainfall
questions.explanation: A drought is an extended period of unusually low rainfall — causing water shortages and dry conditions.

questions.question: The gradual wearing away of rock and soil by water and wind is called:
questions.explanation: Erosion is the gradual wearing away of land surface by natural forces like water, wind, and ice.

questions.question: The layer of gases surrounding the Earth is called the ___. (atmosphere / ozone / sky)
questions.explanation: The atmosphere is the layer of gases surrounding Earth — containing the air we breathe.

questions.question: "Biodiversity" refers to:
options: The study of plants and animals
The variety of life forms in a particular area or on Earth
questions.explanation: Biodiversity means the variety of living organisms in an area — including plants, animals, fungi, and microbes.

questions.question: A "flood" happens when:
options: Too much water covers land that is normally dry
A storm creates very strong winds
questions.explanation: A flood occurs when excess water — from rain, rivers, or sea — covers normally dry land.

questions.question: The natural process that moves water between land, sea, and air is called the water ___. (cycle / system / process)
questions.explanation: The water cycle describes how water evaporates, forms clouds, falls as rain, and flows back to the sea.

questions.question: "Habitat" means:
options: The natural environment where an animal or plant lives
questions.explanation: A habitat is the natural environment in which a particular animal or plant species lives and thrives.

questions.question: "Overcast" weather means:
options: The sky is completely covered in grey cloud
questions.explanation: "Overcast" describes a sky completely covered in grey cloud — dull but not necessarily raining.

questions.question: A large mass of ice that moves slowly over land is called a ___. (glacier / iceberg / ice sheet)
questions.explanation: A glacier is a large, slow-moving mass of ice formed from compacted snow over thousands of years.

questions.question: "Humidity" refers to:
options: The amount of water vapour in the air
The wind speed in an area
The air pressure at sea level
questions.explanation: Humidity measures the amount of water vapour in the air — high humidity makes weather feel hot and sticky.

questions.question: A "tsunami" is:
options: A series of large ocean waves caused by underwater earthquakes
questions.explanation: A tsunami is a series of massive ocean waves triggered by underwater earthquakes or volcanic eruptions.

questions.question: The imaginary line dividing the Earth into north and south is called the ___. (equator / meridian / tropics)
questions.explanation: The equator is the imaginary line at 0° latitude, dividing Earth into the Northern and Southern Hemispheres.

questions.question: "Deforestation" means:
options: The clearing and removal of forests
questions.explanation: Deforestation is the large-scale removal of forests, often for agriculture, logging, or development.

questions.question: "Gale force winds" means:
questions.explanation: Gale force winds are very strong — strong enough to cause damage to trees, buildings, and pose danger.

questions.question: The zone between tropical and polar regions with moderate temperatures is called ___. (temperate / subtropical / moderate)
questions.explanation: Temperate zones have moderate temperatures — not as hot as tropical regions or as cold as polar regions.

questions.question: "Endangered species" refers to animals or plants that:
options: Are at serious risk of becoming extinct
questions.explanation: An endangered species is one whose population has declined so dramatically it risks extinction.

questions.question: The permanent disappearance of a species from Earth is called ___. (extinction / death / elimination)
questions.explanation: Extinction means a species has completely died out — no individuals remain anywhere on Earth.

questions.question: "Carbon footprint" refers to:
options: The physical impact of heavy industry on land
The total amount of greenhouse gases produced by a person or organisation
The area of land damaged by pollution
The amount of electricity used in a building
questions.explanation: A carbon footprint measures the total greenhouse gas emissions caused by an individual, organisation, or product.

questions.question: A vehicle that runs on fixed rails and carries many passengers in a city is called a:
questions.explanation: A tram (or streetcar) runs on rails embedded in city streets and carries passengers in urban areas.

questions.question: The document you need to legally drive a car is called a driving ___. (licence / permit / certificate)
questions.explanation: A driving licence (UK) or driver's license (US) is the legal document permitting you to drive.

questions.question: A "roundabout" is a type of:
options: Road junction where traffic flows in a circle
questions.explanation: A roundabout is a circular road junction where traffic flows in one direction around a central island.

questions.question: "Rush hour" refers to:
options: The fastest speed limit on motorways
The busiest times of day when traffic is heaviest
The time when roads are closed for maintenance
questions.explanation: Rush hour is the peak commuting period — usually morning and evening — when traffic is at its heaviest.

questions.question: A vehicle that carries passengers for a fixed fare and is hailed on the street is called a ___. (taxi / cab / both are correct)
questions.explanation: "Taxi" and "cab" are interchangeable terms for a hired vehicle that carries passengers for a fare.

questions.question: The fee you pay to travel on a bus, train, or other public transport is called the:
questions.explanation: The "fare" is the price you pay to use public transport — a bus fare, train fare, or taxi fare.

questions.question: "Congestion" on roads means:
options: Heavy traffic causing slow movement or standstill
questions.explanation: Congestion is heavy traffic that causes vehicles to slow down significantly or stop completely.

questions.question: A large vehicle for long-distance travel between cities is called a ___. (coach / bus / minibus)
questions.explanation: A coach is a long-distance bus used for intercity or tourist travel — more comfortable than a local bus.

questions.question: A "level crossing" is where:
options: A road passes over a railway line via a bridge
A road and railway cross at the same level
Pedestrians cross a busy road safely
questions.explanation: A level crossing is where a road meets a railway line at the same level — with barriers that lower for trains.

questions.question: "Hybrid" vehicle refers to a car that uses:
options: Both an electric motor and a conventional engine
questions.explanation: A hybrid vehicle combines an electric motor with a traditional petrol or diesel engine for better fuel efficiency.

questions.question: The area where cars can be left temporarily is called a ___ area. (parking / waiting / stopping)
questions.explanation: A parking area or car park is a designated space for leaving vehicles.

questions.question: "Breakdown" of a vehicle means:
options: The vehicle stops working due to a mechanical failure
questions.explanation: A vehicle breakdown means it has stopped working due to mechanical failure — requiring repair or recovery.

questions.question: A "pedestrian" is:
questions.explanation: A pedestrian is anyone travelling on foot — walking or running rather than using a vehicle.

questions.question: The lane on a motorway used only for overtaking faster is called the ___ lane. (fast / overtaking / outside)
questions.explanation: The overtaking lane (outside lane) on a motorway is for passing slower vehicles — not for staying in.

questions.question: "Toll road" means:
options: A free road for all vehicles
A road where you pay a fee to use it
A road restricted to emergency vehicles
A road with speed cameras only
questions.explanation: A toll road requires payment — usually at a booth or automatic barrier — to use that section of road.

questions.question: A "commuter" is someone who:
options: Travels regularly between home and work
Works as a train or bus driver
questions.explanation: A commuter is a person who makes regular, often daily, journeys between their home and workplace.

questions.question: The person who controls the route and safety of a flight is called the ___. (pilot / navigator / captain)
questions.explanation: The pilot (or captain) is responsible for flying the aircraft and ensuring the safety of everyone on board.

questions.question: "Timetable" for transport refers to:
options: The speed limits on different roads
A schedule showing departure and arrival times
The list of stations or stops on a route
The pricing structure for different journey types
questions.explanation: A timetable is a schedule showing when transport services depart and arrive at each stop.

questions.question: A vehicle used to carry goods rather than passengers is called a ___. (van / truck / lorry — all are correct)
questions.explanation: Trucks (US), lorries (UK), and vans are all vehicles designed primarily for transporting goods.

questions.question: "Road rage" refers to:
options: Aggressive or violent behaviour by drivers towards other road users
questions.explanation: Road rage is when a driver becomes extremely angry and behaves aggressively towards other road users.

questions.question: "Revenue" for a business means:
options: The total costs of running the business
The total income earned from sales and services
The profit after all expenses are paid
questions.explanation: Revenue is the total money a business earns from its activities before any costs are deducted.

questions.question: Money left over after all costs are deducted from revenue is called ___. (profit / revenue / income)
questions.explanation: Profit is what remains after all business costs and expenses are subtracted from total revenue.

questions.question: "Inflation" means:
options: A fall in the general level of prices
A rise in the general level of prices over time
The growth of a country's economy
questions.explanation: Inflation is the rate at which the general level of prices rises over time, reducing purchasing power.

questions.question: "Budget" refers to:
options: A plan showing expected income and expenditure over a period
questions.explanation: A budget is a financial plan — estimating income and expenses for a set period to manage money effectively.

questions.question: Money owed by one person or organisation to another is called ___. (debt / loan / credit)
questions.explanation: "Debt" is money that is owed — borrowed and not yet repaid.

questions.question: "Dividend" refers to:
options: Interest paid on a savings account
A payment made to shareholders from company profits
questions.explanation: A dividend is a share of company profits paid to shareholders — a reward for investing in the company.

questions.question: "Exchange rate" means:
options: The interest rate on foreign loans
The rate at which one currency can be exchanged for another
The fee charged for international transfers
The price of gold and commodities
questions.explanation: The exchange rate is how much of one currency you get for another — for example, pounds to dollars.

questions.question: Money paid by workers to the government based on their income is called income ___. (tax / duty / levy)
questions.explanation: Income tax is money paid to the government from your earnings — usually calculated as a percentage.

questions.question: "Invoice" is a document that:
options: Requests payment for goods or services provided
questions.explanation: An invoice is a bill — a request for payment from a business to a customer for goods or services.

questions.question: "Capital" in business refers to:
options: The country where the business is based
Money or assets used to start or run a business
The annual profit of a company
The number of employees in a company
questions.explanation: Capital refers to money or financial assets available to a business for investment and operations.

questions.question: The money you put into an investment hoping to make more money is called ___. (capital / principal / investment)
questions.explanation: An investment is money put into something — shares, property, or a business — to generate future returns.

questions.question: "Subsidy" means:
options: A tax penalty for a business
Financial support given by a government to reduce costs
A business loan from a bank
questions.explanation: A subsidy is money given by a government to support a business or industry, or to keep prices lower.

questions.question: "Cash flow" refers to:
options: The total profit of a business in a year
The movement of money into and out of a business
The amount of money a company owes its suppliers
The value of all a company's assets
questions.explanation: Cash flow is the movement of money in (income) and out (expenses) of a business — crucial for day-to-day operations.

questions.question: Money given to someone for a specific purpose that must be repaid is called a ___. (loan / grant / subsidy)
questions.explanation: A loan is money borrowed that must be repaid, usually with interest, over an agreed period.

questions.question: "Recession" means:
options: A period of rapid economic growth
A period of economic decline with reduced output and employment
questions.explanation: A recession is a period of economic decline — typically two consecutive quarters of negative economic growth.

questions.question: "Expenditure" means:
options: Money spent on goods and services
Money saved in a bank account
questions.explanation: Expenditure is money spent — the total amount paid out for goods, services, or other costs.

questions.question: A person or organisation that is owed money is called a ___. (creditor / debtor / lender)
questions.explanation: A creditor is owed money. A debtor owes money. A lender provides loans.

questions.question: "Compound interest" means:
options: Interest calculated only on the original amount borrowed or saved
Interest calculated on both the original amount and previously earned interest
A fixed monthly fee charged by a bank
questions.explanation: Compound interest means you earn interest on your interest — causing savings or debts to grow exponentially.

questions.question: The ability to convert an asset into cash quickly is called ___. (liquidity / flexibility / accessibility)
questions.explanation: "Liquidity" refers to how easily an asset can be converted to cash — cash itself is the most liquid asset.

questions.question: "Net worth" means:
options: The total value of what you own minus what you owe
The market value of a company's shares
questions.explanation: Net worth is the total value of all your assets (what you own) minus all your liabilities (what you owe).

questions.question: Someone described as "resilient" is:
options: Able to recover quickly from difficulties
questions.explanation: "Resilient" means able to recover quickly and adapt well after adversity or setbacks.

questions.question: A person who is always truthful and direct is described as ___. (honest / frank / both are correct)
questions.explanation: "Honest" means truthful. "Frank" means direct and straightforward. Both are positive character traits.

questions.question: "Empathetic" means:
options: Able to understand and share the feelings of others
Being overly concerned with your own feelings
Being calm and unemotional in difficult situations
questions.explanation: "Empathetic" means you can understand and share how others feel — a deeply valued social quality.

questions.question: A "compassionate" person is:
options: Showing concern and care for the suffering of others
Strict and firm in their decisions
questions.explanation: "Compassionate" means feeling and showing care and concern for others who are suffering.

questions.question: Someone who is overly worried about their health is described as ___. (hypochondriac / anxious / health-conscious)
questions.explanation: A hypochondriac is someone excessively and unnecessarily worried about having a serious illness.

questions.question: "Frugal" describes someone who:
options: Spends money very freely and generously
Is careful and economical with money, not wasteful
questions.explanation: "Frugal" means economical with money — avoiding waste and unnecessary spending.

questions.question: "Tenacious" means:
options: Easily discouraged and giving up quickly
Persistent and determined — not giving up easily
Shy and reluctant to speak up
questions.explanation: "Tenacious" means determined and persistent — holding firmly to a purpose despite difficulties.

questions.question: A person who always sees the best possible outcome is described as ___. (optimistic / positive / hopeful)
questions.explanation: "Optimistic" means expecting the best outcome — the opposite of "pessimistic."

questions.question: "Condescending" behaviour means:
options: Being respectful to people below your level
Treating people as though they are inferior or less intelligent
Being critical but fair in giving feedback
questions.explanation: "Condescending" means treating others as inferior — talking down to them or being patronising.

questions.question: A person described as "meticulous" is:
options: Extremely careful and precise about details
questions.explanation: "Meticulous" means very careful, precise, and thorough — paying close attention to every detail.

questions.question: Someone who enjoys being with others and gains energy from social situations is called ___. (extrovert / social / outgoing)
questions.explanation: An extrovert gains energy from being around people — the opposite of an introvert who prefers time alone.

questions.question: "Assertive" means:
options: Quietly confident — expressing needs and opinions clearly and respectfully
questions.explanation: "Assertive" is positively confident — expressing yourself clearly without being aggressive or passive.

questions.question: "Cynical" describes someone who:
options: Believes people are always motivated by self-interest — distrusting others' motives
Is very idealistic about the world
questions.explanation: "Cynical" means distrustful — believing most people act out of selfish motives rather than genuine goodness.

questions.question: A person who strongly believes in fairness and equal rights is described as ___. (principled / moral / ethical)
questions.explanation: "Principled" means having and following strong moral principles — acting according to what is right.

questions.question: "Spontaneous" behaviour means:
options: Very planned and organised in advance
Done on impulse without planning — natural and unscripted
questions.explanation: "Spontaneous" means happening naturally without prior planning — done on impulse in the moment.

questions.question: "Indifferent" means:
options: Having no particular interest or concern
questions.explanation: "Indifferent" means not caring either way — showing no interest or concern in something.

questions.question: A person who is easily frightened or nervous is described as ___. (timid / shy / nervous)
questions.explanation: "Timid" means lacking confidence and easily frightened — more specific than "shy" which is social awkwardness.

questions.question: "Gregarious" means:
options: Fond of company — sociable and enjoying being with others
Formal and reserved in social situations
questions.explanation: "Gregarious" means naturally sociable and enjoying the company of others — going beyond just being "friendly."

questions.question: Someone who takes pleasure in others' misfortune is described as ___. (schadenfreude / spiteful / malicious)
questions.explanation: "Spiteful" means deliberately unkind or harmful. "Schadenfreude" is a specific feeling of pleasure at others' misfortune.

questions.question: "Pragmatic" describes someone who:
options: Focuses on practical solutions rather than theories or ideals
Is inflexible and rigid in their thinking
Makes decisions based purely on emotions
questions.explanation: "Pragmatic" means practical and focused on what works in reality — rather than idealistic or theoretical.

questions.question: "Software" refers to:
options: The physical parts of a computer
Programs and applications that run on a computer or device
The screen and keyboard of a device
The internet connection used by a device
questions.explanation: Software is the programs, applications, and operating systems that run on hardware devices.

questions.question: The physical parts of a computer — like the screen, keyboard, and processor — are called ___. (hardware / software / components)
questions.explanation: Hardware refers to the physical, tangible parts of a computer or device that you can touch.

questions.question: "Cloud storage" means:
options: Storing files on a USB drive
Storing files on physical hard drives at home
Storing files on remote internet servers accessed online
Storing files automatically on your device
questions.explanation: Cloud storage means your files are stored on remote servers and accessed via the internet from any device.

questions.question: "Bandwidth" in internet terms refers to:
options: The physical cable connecting to the internet
The maximum amount of data that can be transmitted in a given time
The price of your internet subscription
The distance from your router to your device
questions.explanation: Bandwidth is the maximum data transfer rate — higher bandwidth means faster internet speeds.

questions.question: An attempt by criminals to trick you into revealing personal information via a fake email is called ___. (phishing / hacking / scamming)
questions.explanation: "Phishing" is a cybercrime where fraudulent emails or websites trick people into revealing passwords or financial details.

questions.question: "Algorithm" in computing means:
options: A set of rules or instructions a computer follows to solve a problem
The speed at which a computer operates
questions.explanation: An algorithm is a defined sequence of rules or instructions that a computer follows to complete a task.

questions.question: "Encryption" means:
options: Deleting data permanently from a device
Converting data into a coded form to prevent unauthorised access
Compressing files to save storage space
Backing up files to an external drive
questions.explanation: Encryption converts data into a coded format — only someone with the correct key can read it.

questions.question: Harmful software designed to damage or gain unauthorised access to a system is called ___. (malware / spyware / virus)
questions.explanation: "Malware" (malicious software) is a broad term for software designed to harm systems — including viruses and spyware.

questions.question: "Artificial intelligence" (AI) refers to:
options: Computer systems that simulate human intelligence and learning
The internet infrastructure connecting devices globally
questions.explanation: AI is computer systems designed to perform tasks that normally require human intelligence — like learning and problem-solving.

questions.question: "Update" or "upgrade" your device software means:
options: Install a newer version of the software to fix bugs and add features
Back up your data to the cloud
Reset your device to factory settings
questions.explanation: Updating software installs a newer version — fixing security vulnerabilities, bugs, and adding improvements.

questions.question: Connecting to a computer network without physical cables is called ___ connection. (wireless / bluetooth / wifi)
questions.explanation: "Wireless" or "Wi-Fi" means internet connection without physical cables — using radio signals.

questions.question: "User interface" (UI) refers to:
options: The speed and performance of an application
The visual design and controls through which users interact with software
The programming code that makes software work
The security features protecting user data
questions.explanation: A user interface (UI) is the visual layout — buttons, menus, and screens — that allow users to interact with software.

questions.question: "Streaming" media means:
options: Watching or listening to content in real time over the internet
Sharing media files with other users
Creating video content for online platforms
questions.explanation: Streaming means consuming audio or video content in real time over the internet — without downloading.

questions.question: The set of rules that define how devices communicate over a network is called a ___. (protocol / system / network)
questions.explanation: A protocol is a standardised set of rules for transmitting data between devices on a network.

questions.question: "Two-factor authentication" (2FA) provides security by:
options: Requiring a very long and complex password
Requiring two forms of verification to access an account
Changing your password automatically every month
questions.explanation: 2FA requires two verification steps — usually a password and a code sent to your phone — for added security.

questions.question: "Data breach" means:
options: Unauthorised access to private or confidential data
Corrupted files that cannot be opened
Slow internet connection affecting data transfer
questions.explanation: A data breach is when unauthorised individuals gain access to private or sensitive information.

questions.question: Copying files to a separate location to prevent losing them is called making a ___. (backup / copy / duplicate)
questions.explanation: A backup is a copy of your data stored separately — so you can recover it if the original is lost or damaged.

questions.question: "Virtual reality" (VR) refers to:
options: A simulated three-dimensional environment experienced through a headset
Augmented digital images overlaid on the real world
questions.explanation: VR creates a fully immersive simulated environment viewed through a headset — used in gaming, training, and more.

questions.question: The main circuit board inside a computer that connects all components is called the ___. (motherboard / processor / circuit)
questions.explanation: The motherboard is the main printed circuit board that connects the CPU, memory, and all other components.

questions.question: "Open source" software means:
options: Software that is free to download but not to modify
Software whose source code is publicly available for anyone to view, use, and modify
Software developed exclusively by large technology companies
Software that requires an internet connection to function
questions.explanation: Open source software has publicly available code that anyone can view, modify, and distribute freely.

questions.question: In football, "offside" means:
options: The ball goes out of bounds
A player is in an illegal position closer to the opponent's goal than the ball
The goalkeeper touches the ball outside the penalty area
questions.explanation: "Offside" in football is when an attacking player is between the opponents' goal and the last defender when the ball is played.

questions.question: Swimming using only your arms while your body is face-down is called the front ___. (crawl / stroke / paddle)
questions.explanation: The front crawl (or freestyle) is the fastest swimming stroke — alternating arm pulls with a flutter kick.

questions.question: In cricket, a "century" means a player has scored:
options: Won the match for their team
questions.explanation: A century in cricket means a batsman has scored 100 or more runs in a single innings.

questions.question: "Marathon" running refers to a race of approximately:
questions.explanation: A marathon is a race of 42.195 kilometres — named after the ancient Greek city of Marathon.

questions.question: Creating pictures or designs using pencils, charcoal, or pens is called ___. (drawing / sketching / both are correct)
questions.explanation: "Drawing" and "sketching" both refer to creating images with pencils or pens — sketching is often more loose and quick.

questions.question: "Stamina" in sport means:
options: The ability to sustain physical activity over a long period
questions.explanation: "Stamina" is the ability to maintain effort over a prolonged period — essential for endurance sports.

questions.question: "Amateur" in sport means:
options: Someone who plays sport professionally for money
Someone who plays sport for enjoyment without being paid
Someone who has just started learning a sport
Someone who is very skilled at their sport
questions.explanation: "Amateur" means participating in sport for pleasure without payment — the opposite of "professional."

questions.question: A hobby that involves growing vegetables and flowers at home is called ___. (gardening / farming / horticulture)
questions.explanation: Gardening is the hobby of cultivating plants — growing vegetables, flowers, and herbs at home.

questions.question: In a relay race, what is a "baton"?
options: A small stick passed between team members
The finishing tape at the end of the race
questions.explanation: A baton is the stick passed from runner to runner in a relay race — if dropped, the team is often disqualified.

questions.question: "Mindfulness" as a hobby involves:
options: Paying deliberate, calm attention to the present moment
A form of intense physical exercise
A social group activity involving breathing exercises
questions.explanation: Mindfulness as a hobby means practising present-moment awareness — often through meditation or mindful activities.

questions.question: The competitive cycling event known for its multi-day stage races across France is the Tour de ___. (France / Cycling / Vélo)
questions.explanation: The Tour de France is the world's most famous cycling race — a multi-stage race held annually across France.

questions.question: "Knitting" as a hobby involves:
options: Cutting fabric into patterns to make clothes
Using needles and yarn to create fabric or garments
questions.explanation: Knitting uses two needles and yarn to create fabric — producing garments like scarves, hats, and jumpers.

questions.question: A "draw" or "tie" in sport means:
options: Both teams or players finished with the same score
The match was abandoned due to bad weather
One player or team was disqualified
questions.explanation: A draw or tie means both sides finished the competition with an equal score — no winner.

questions.question: The hobby of creating models of historical scenes and events in miniature is called ___. (diorama-making / modelling / crafting)
questions.explanation: "Modelling" or "scale modelling" involves building detailed miniature representations of vehicles, figures, or scenes.

questions.question: "Personal best" (PB) in sport refers to:
options: Your favourite sport or athletic event
The best performance you have personally achieved in a specific event
A national record in an athletic discipline
A score set by a professional athlete you admire
questions.explanation: A personal best (PB) or personal record (PR) is the best result you have personally achieved in an event.

questions.question: "Choreography" in dance refers to:
options: The music used for a dance performance
The art of designing and arranging the movements in a dance
The costume and costume design for performers
The stage lighting used during a dance show
questions.explanation: Choreography is the art of designing and sequencing dance movements — the choreographer creates the routine.

questions.question: The hobby of collecting and trading playing cards or game cards is called ___. (card collecting / trading card collecting / both are correct)
questions.explanation: Both "card collecting" and "trading card collecting" are used to describe this hobby.

questions.question: "Cardio" exercise refers to:
options: Aerobic exercise that raises your heart rate for sustained periods
Short intense bursts of maximum effort
questions.explanation: "Cardio" (cardiovascular exercise) includes activities like running, cycling, and swimming that raise your heart rate.

questions.question: The hobby of observing and recording different species of birds in their natural habitat is called bird ___. (watching / spotting / both are correct)
questions.explanation: "Birdwatching" and "birdspotting" are both used — "birdwatching" is more common in British English.

questions.question: "Podium" finish in sport means:
options: Finishing in first, second, or third place
Achieving a personal best time or score
questions.explanation: A "podium finish" means finishing in the top three positions — where athletes stand on the award podium.
